Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 63-year-old man is seen 6 weeks after curative-intent right hepatectomy and regional lymphadenectomy for a 4.5-cm intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma discovered during evaluation of cholestatic liver enzyme elevations. Final pathology shows a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, pT2N1, with negative margins (R0) and 1 of 8 lymph nodes positive for metastasis. Postoperative labs show total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, AST 42 U/L, ALT 35 U/L, and alkaline phosphatase 118 U/L. ECOG performance status is 0. He asks about next steps to reduce the risk of recurrence.

According to the Spanish Society of Medical Oncology (SEOM), Spanish Multidisciplinary Group of Digestive Cancer (GEMCAD), and Digestive Tumor Treatment Group (TTD) clinical practice guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate adjuvant capecitabine for eight cycles
Answer.Choice_B: Observation with surveillance imaging and laboratory monitoring only
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate adjuvant gemcitabine plus cisplatin for six cycles
Answer.Choice_D: Initiate adjuvant fluoropyrimidine-based chemoradiotherapy to the hepatic hilum and regional lymphatics
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SEOM–GEMCAD–TTD guideline endorses adjuvant chemotherapy with eight cycles of capecitabine for patients with biliary tract cancer after curative-intent resection, based on the BILCAP trial showing improved survival with capecitabine versus observation. Earlier guidance (2015) advised against adjuvant therapy due to lack of benefit, making observation (B) reflect outdated practice. Gemcitabine–cisplatin (C) is standard for advanced/unresectable disease and is not recommended in the adjuvant setting. Routine adjuvant chemoradiotherapy (D) is not recommended after an R0 resection and is generally reserved for select cases such as positive margins; it is not the recommended standard for R0 resected disease.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 64-year-old man with unresectable intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma presents after radiographic progression while receiving first-line cisplatin plus gemcitabine; he completed 8 cycles over 6 months with initial stable disease followed by progression. He has an ECOG performance status of 1 and good organ function (bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, ANC and platelets within normal limits). He reports mild numbness in his fingertips that does not interfere with activities. Next-generation sequencing shows no actionable alterations (no FGFR2 fusion, no IDH1 mutation, HER2 negative); the tumor is microsatellite stable with low tumor mutational burden. He asks about further treatment options.

According to the 2025 joint clinical practice guideline from the Spanish Society of Medical Oncology (SEOM), the Spanish Multidisciplinary Group of Digestive Cancer (GEMCAD), and the Digestive Tumor Treatment Group (TTD), which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate FOLFOX (oxaliplatin plus 5-fluorouracil/leucovorin) as second-line systemic therapy
Answer.Choice_B: Provide active supportive care without administering second-line cytotoxic chemotherapy
Answer.Choice_C: Switch to FOLFIRI (irinotecan plus 5-fluorouracil/leucovorin) as second-line systemic therapy
Answer.Choice_D: Start pembrolizumab monotherapy as second-line therapy regardless of microsatellite status
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SEOM–GEMCAD–TTD guideline recommends FOLFOX as the standard second-line regimen for fit patients with advanced biliary tract cancer that has progressed after first-line cisplatin/gemcitabine, based on the phase III ABC-06 trial showing improved overall survival versus active supportive care. Earlier guidance (2015) stated there was insufficient evidence to recommend second-line chemotherapy, favoring supportive care. FOLFIRI is not established as a standard second-line regimen, and pembrolizumab is not recommended for microsatellite-stable disease without targetable alterations.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 63-year-old woman presents 6 weeks after R0 resection of a pT2N0 gallbladder carcinoma. She completed an uncomplicated postoperative recovery and is asymptomatic. Physical examination is unremarkable, and baseline labs obtained today show normal liver function tests. She asks about a structured follow-up plan to detect recurrence early. According to the Spanish Society of Medical Oncology (SEOM), Spanish Multidisciplinary Group of Digestive Cancer (GEMCAD), and Digestive Tumor Treatment Group (TTD) guideline issued in 2025, which of the following surveillance strategies is recommended after curative treatment for biliary tract cancer?

Answer.Choice_A: Schedule visits every 3–6 months during the first 2 years that include clinical examination, laboratory tests with CEA and CA 19-9, and contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax, abdomen, and pelvis, then transition to annual visits through year 5
Answer.Choice_B: Avoid routine follow-up; if follow-up is undertaken, limit it to history and physical examination, reserving laboratory tests and imaging for when there is clinical suspicion of recurrence
Answer.Choice_C: Obtain abdominal ultrasound and CA 19-9 every 6 months for 5 years without cross-sectional chest imaging or CEA measurement
Answer.Choice_D: Perform PET-CT of the whole body every 3 months for 2 years with monthly CEA and CA 19-9, then discontinue surveillance thereafter
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SEOM–GEMCAD–TTD guideline recommends structured surveillance after curative therapy for biliary tract cancers due to high early recurrence risk: clinic visits every 3–6 months for the first 2 years including clinical exam, laboratory tests, tumor markers (CEA, CA 19-9), and CT of the thorax/abdomen/pelvis, then annually until 5 years. Earlier guidance (2015) advised against routine follow-up beyond history/physical unless there was clinical suspicion, making option B reflective of the older approach. Options C and D are not recommended surveillance strategies in these guidelines.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 67-year-old man is evaluated for newly diagnosed unresectable, metastatic hilar cholangiocarcinoma. He initially presented with jaundice and pruritus; ERCP with biliary stent placement improved his bilirubin. Biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma consistent with biliary tract origin. CT shows multifocal hepatic lesions and small bilateral pulmonary nodules. ECOG performance status is 1. Past history includes chronic hepatitis B on entecavir with undetectable HBV DNA, hypertension, and no autoimmune disease. He has no baseline neuropathy or hearing loss. Laboratory studies show total bilirubin 1.7 mg/dL, AST 48 U/L, ALT 52 U/L, albumin 3.8 g/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR 88 mL/min/1.73 m²). Tumor testing shows microsatellite stability and PD-L1 TPS 0%. He asks about the optimal first-line systemic therapy.

According to the 2025 clinical practice guideline from the Spanish Society of Medical Oncology (SEOM), the Spanish Multidisciplinary Group of Digestive Cancer (GEMCAD), and the Digestive Tumor Treatment Group (TTD), which of the following is the most appropriate first-line regimen for this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Cisplatin plus gemcitabine combined with an immune-checkpoint inhibitor (eg, durvalumab or pembrolizumab)
Answer.Choice_B: Cisplatin plus gemcitabine without immunotherapy
Answer.Choice_C: Oxaliplatin, leucovorin, and 5-fluorouracil (FOLFOX) combined with nivolumab
Answer.Choice_D: Gemcitabine plus oxaliplatin combined with pembrolizumab
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SEOM–GEMCAD–TTD guideline designates cisplatin and gemcitabine combined with an immune-checkpoint inhibitor (durvalumab or pembrolizumab) as the standard first-line regimen for unresectable locally advanced or metastatic biliary tract cancer in patients without contraindications to immunotherapy. This recommendation reflects evidence from TOPAZ-1 (durvalumab + GemCis) and KEYNOTE-966 (pembrolizumab + GemCis). In patients with immunotherapy contraindications, cisplatin plus gemcitabine alone may be used; in those unable to receive cisplatin, gemcitabine monotherapy or gemcitabine with oxaliplatin are alternatives, but adding an ICI to GemOx is not guideline-recommended. Therefore, adding an ICI to GemCis is preferred regardless of PD-L1 status in this fit patient with adequate renal function and no autoimmune disease.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 54-year-old man presents for multidisciplinary evaluation of newly diagnosed, resectable stage IIIC cutaneous melanoma. Four weeks ago, he underwent wide local excision of a 3.1-mm ulcerated melanoma of the posterior calf with a positive sentinel lymph node. PET/CT now shows multiple ipsilateral popliteal and inguinal lymph nodes measuring up to 3.5 cm without distant metastases. Brain MRI is negative. He has an ECOG performance status of 0 and no history of autoimmune disease. BRAF mutation testing is pending. The surgical team believes complete lymphadenectomy is feasible but morbid. 

According to the Society for Immunotherapy of Cancer guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate neoadjuvant ipilimumab plus nivolumab after multidisciplinary discussion, with adjuvant therapy determined by pathologic response following resection
Answer.Choice_B: Defer neoadjuvant therapy in favor of upfront lymph node dissection; consider neoadjuvant pembrolizumab only as an optional approach after multidisciplinary discussion
Answer.Choice_C: Proceed directly to surgery followed by adjuvant nivolumab; neoadjuvant therapy is not recommended in resectable stage III melanoma without brain metastases
Answer.Choice_D: Initiate neoadjuvant relatlimab plus nivolumab with adjuvant continuation regardless of pathologic response
Answer.Explanation: SITC 2025 recommends that neoadjuvant therapy should be considered for resectable stage IIIB–IV melanoma without brain metastases. Acceptable options include pembrolizumab with adjuvant continuation (phase II evidence) and ipilimumab plus nivolumab with response-driven adjuvant therapy (phase III evidence). Option A reflects this update. Option B aligns with earlier guidance that neoadjuvant therapy may be considered but was not emphasized. Options C and D do not reflect SITC-endorsed neoadjuvant strategies for this setting.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 62-year-old man with unresectable metastatic cutaneous melanoma presents after radiographic and symptomatic progression on pembrolizumab monotherapy. He has not received anti–CTLA-4 therapy. Tumor genotyping shows BRAF V600 wild type. Physical examination reveals a 4-cm right axillary nodal mass and several subcentimeter pulmonary nodules. ECOG performance status is 1. Laboratory studies show normal renal and hepatic function. Surgical oncology notes the axillary mass is safely resectable for tissue procurement.

According to the Society for Immunotherapy of Cancer guideline issued in 2025, which of the following interventions is newly included as a subsequent-line option for patients like this after progression on anti–PD-1-based therapy without prior anti–CTLA-4?

Answer.Choice_A: Autologous tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte therapy with lifileucel following surgical tumor procurement
Answer.Choice_B: Adoptive cell therapy with tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes only within a clinical trial
Answer.Choice_C: Switch to nivolumab plus relatlimab after progression on pembrolizumab
Answer.Choice_D: Combination ipilimumab plus nivolumab
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 Society for Immunotherapy of Cancer guideline adds lifileucel, an autologous tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy, as a named subsequent-line treatment option after progression on anti–PD-1-based therapy without prior anti–CTLA-4 exposure. In 2023 guidance, adoptive TIL therapies were encouraged primarily via clinical trials without specifying a product. Ipilimumab plus nivolumab remains an option across versions but is not newly added; switching to another anti–PD-1-based regimen such as nivolumab–relatlimab after PD-1 progression is not recommended as a specific added option in this context.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 54-year-old woman with metastatic cutaneous melanoma (BRAF V600 wild-type) presents after radiographic progression following 3 months of pembrolizumab monotherapy. Her best response was progressive disease despite at least 6 weeks of therapy. During treatment, she developed grade 3 immune-mediated colitis requiring high-dose corticosteroids and infliximab; symptoms have resolved and steroids were tapered off 6 weeks ago. She has an ECOG performance status of 1, preserved hepatic and renal function, and a surgically accessible soft-tissue lesion. She lives in a remote area and clinical trial enrollment is not feasible. 

According to the Society for Immunotherapy of Cancer guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

Answer.Choice_A: Adoptive cell therapy with lifileucel
Answer.Choice_B: Combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab plus nivolumab
Answer.Choice_C: Ipilimumab monotherapy
Answer.Choice_D: Re-induction with single-agent anti–PD-1 therapy
Answer.Explanation: This patient has primary resistance to anti–PD-1 therapy (best response progressive disease after adequate exposure) and a history of severe immune-related colitis, making additional checkpoint blockade particularly risky. The 2025 Society for Immunotherapy of Cancer guideline adds adoptive cell therapy with lifileucel as a recommended option when clinical trials are not feasible and elevates lifileucel to a preferred option alongside ipilimumab plus nivolumab for primary anti–PD-1 resistance. In this scenario, lifileucel is the most appropriate next step given efficacy in PD-1–refractory melanoma and the patient’s contraindication to further immune checkpoint therapy. Combination ipilimumab plus nivolumab (prior preferred option) remains a consideration but is less appropriate here due to high risk of recurrent severe colitis. Ipilimumab monotherapy is less effective than the combination and carries similar toxicity concerns. Re-induction with anti–PD-1 is considered for secondary resistance after initial benefit, not for primary resistance.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 9-year-old boy is seen for follow-up of chronic pruritic eczema affecting the antecubital and popliteal fossae. He has moderate atopic dermatitis with sleep-disrupting itch despite consistent emollient use and intermittent medium-potency topical corticosteroids for flares. There is no evidence of secondary infection. He is otherwise healthy, takes no daily medications, and has a balanced diet. His parents ask whether adding probiotics or prebiotics might help his symptoms.

According to the Korean Atopic Dermatitis Association guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is recommended regarding the use of probiotics/prebiotics in this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Offer a limited trial of oral probiotics or prebiotics as adjunctive therapy to help relieve symptoms
Answer.Choice_B: Avoid recommending probiotics or prebiotics for treatment or prevention and maintain a neutral stance due to insufficient evidence
Answer.Choice_C: Recommend daily multispecies oral probiotics for 6–12 months as disease-modifying therapy to prevent future flares
Answer.Choice_D: Use topical probiotic-containing preparations on affected skin as part of first-line anti-inflammatory management
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 Korean Atopic Dermatitis Association guideline proposes the limited use of probiotics/prebiotics for symptomatic relief in patients with atopic dermatitis, supporting an adjunctive, trial-based approach rather than routine or disease-modifying use. In contrast, the 2015 position adopted a neutral stance and did not endorse probiotics/prebiotics for treatment or prevention. There is no recommendation for routine long-term multispecies probiotic therapy as a disease-modifying strategy, and topical probiotic preparations are not part of first-line anti-inflammatory management. Citation: Korean Atopic Dermatitis Association guideline, 2025.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 17-year-old girl with a history of mild-to-moderate atopic dermatitis presents during winter for increased pruritus and dryness despite regular use of fragrance-free emollients and intermittent low- to mid-potency topical corticosteroids. She has no other medical conditions and takes no medications. Examination shows lichenified eczematous plaques in the antecubital fossae with excoriations and xerosis; there is no evidence of secondary infection. She asks about over-the-counter evening primrose oil capsules recommended by a friend and wonders whether they might help with itch and dryness. According to the Korean Atopic Dermatitis Association guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate counseling?

Answer.Choice_A: Offer limited use of evening primrose oil as an adjunct for symptomatic relief, while continuing standard skin-directed therapy
Answer.Choice_B: Advise against evening primrose oil or any essential fatty acid supplements for atopic dermatitis due to insufficient evidence
Answer.Choice_C: Recommend routine omega-3 docosahexaenoic acid supplementation as first-line adjunct therapy for atopic dermatitis symptoms
Answer.Choice_D: Prescribe high-dose evening primrose oil as disease-modifying therapy to reduce flares and minimize need for topical corticosteroids
Answer.Explanation: According to the Korean Atopic Dermatitis Association guideline issued in 2025, limited use of evening primrose oil can be offered for symptomatic relief in atopic dermatitis. This represents a shift from earlier guidance (2015) that did not recommend essential fatty acids due to insufficient randomized controlled trial evidence. Evening primrose oil may alleviate dryness and pruritus for some patients, but it is not disease-modifying and should be used as an adjunct to standard therapies. Recommending against all essential fatty acids (B) reflects earlier guidance. Routine omega-3 (docosahexaenoic acid) supplementation (C) and high-dose evening primrose oil as disease-modifying therapy (D) are not recommended.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 28-year-old woman with a 10-year history of moderate atopic dermatitis presents in winter with increased pruritus and eczematous plaques in the antecubital and popliteal fossae. She has been adherent to emollients and intermittent medium-potency topical corticosteroids with partial relief. She asks whether taking an over-the-counter vitamin D supplement could help her symptoms as an additional low-risk measure. She has no history of nephrolithiasis, granulomatous disease, or hypercalcemia, and takes no other medications. According to the Korean Atopic Dermatitis Association clinical practice guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate counseling regarding vitamin D?

Answer.Choice_A: Offer a limited course of oral vitamin D supplementation as an adjunct for symptomatic relief
Answer.Choice_B: Neither recommend nor discourage vitamin D supplementation, given inconsistent evidence, and maintain a neutral stance
Answer.Choice_C: Recommend routine high-dose oral vitamin D supplementation for all patients with atopic dermatitis to reduce flares
Answer.Choice_D: Advise against any vitamin D supplementation in atopic dermatitis because potential harms outweigh benefits
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 Korean Atopic Dermatitis Association guideline proposes the limited use of vitamin D as an adjunct for symptomatic relief in patients with atopic dermatitis. Earlier guidance (2015) characterized vitamin D as a low-risk adjunct with contradictory efficacy data and took a neutral stance. Therefore, offering limited adjunctive vitamin D aligns with the updated recommendation, whereas maintaining neutrality (B), recommending routine high-dose supplementation (C), or advising against any use (D) do not reflect the 2025 guidance.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 26-year-old woman with a 3-year history of systemic lupus erythematosus presents with 3 weeks of progressive lower-extremity edema and frothy urine. She takes hydroxychloroquine and low-dose prednisone but no other immunosuppressants. Blood pressure is 138/84 mm Hg. Urinalysis shows 3+ protein and dysmorphic red blood cells with numerous red blood cell casts. Spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio is 4.2 g/g. Serum creatinine is 1.1 mg/dL (baseline 0.8 mg/dL). Complement C3 is low, anti–double-stranded DNA antibody titer is markedly elevated, and serologies for hepatitis B and C are negative. Kidney biopsy shows active class IV (A/C) lupus nephritis with concurrent membranous features (class V). She strongly wishes to preserve fertility and asks about induction options that avoid cyclophosphamide. According to the 2025 guideline from the National Clinical Research Center for Dermatologic and Immunologic Diseases (Peking Union Medical College Hospital), Chinese Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Treatment and Research Group, and collaborating Chinese rheumatology societies, which of the following induction strategies is an acceptable option beyond mycophenolate mofetil or cyclophosphamide?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate high-dose glucocorticoids in combination with mycophenolate mofetil and tacrolimus (multi-target regimen)
Answer.Choice_B: Initiate high-dose glucocorticoids with either mycophenolate mofetil or intravenous cyclophosphamide only, without calcineurin inhibitors or biologics
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate high-dose glucocorticoids with rituximab as first-line induction
Answer.Choice_D: Initiate high-dose glucocorticoids with belimumab alone, without mycophenolate mofetil or cyclophosphamide
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 Chinese multidisciplinary guideline for lupus nephritis expands induction options for active class III/IV (±V) disease to include glucocorticoids combined with (a) MMF or IV cyclophosphamide, which may be combined with belimumab; (b) a calcineurin inhibitor; or (c) MMF plus a calcineurin inhibitor (multi-target). Thus, glucocorticoids with MMF and tacrolimus is an endorsed induction option. The earlier standard emphasized only MMF or cyclophosphamide with glucocorticoids (Choice B). Rituximab is reserved for persistent or refractory disease, not routine first-line induction (Choice C). Belimumab monotherapy without MMF or cyclophosphamide is not recommended for induction (Choice D).
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 34-year-old woman with a 6-year history of systemic lupus erythematosus presents with hematemesis and melena. She is pale and fatigued but hemodynamically stable. Examination shows diffuse oral petechiae and oozing from gingiva. Laboratory testing reveals platelets 3,000/µL, hemoglobin 7.4 g/dL, normal PT/INR and aPTT, and no schistocytes on peripheral smear. Coombs testing is negative; creatinine and complements are at her lupus baseline. She has been diagnosed with SLE-associated immune thrombocytopenia. Urgent upper endoscopy for hemostasis is scheduled. She has received platelet transfusions, high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g), and intravenous immunoglobulin (1 g/kg) with a repeated platelet count of 4,000/µL after 6 hours. 

According to the guideline jointly issued in 2025 by the National Clinical Research Center for Dermatologic and Immunologic Diseases (Peking Union Medical College Hospital), the Chinese Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Treatment and Research Group, the Chinese Rheumatology Association, and collaborating societies, which of the following interventions should be initiated now to minimize bleeding risk and facilitate the urgent procedure?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate a thrombopoietin receptor agonist (eg, romiplostim or eltrombopag) in addition to ongoing therapy
Answer.Choice_B: Administer rituximab urgently as rescue therapy for life-threatening hematologic involvement
Answer.Choice_C: Proceed with emergent splenectomy to achieve rapid platelet recovery
Answer.Choice_D: Begin tranexamic acid monotherapy without further immunomodulation
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 Chinese multisociety SLE guideline specifies that for SLE-associated immune thrombocytopenia, high-dose glucocorticoids and/or IVIG are used acutely, and thrombopoietin receptor agonists can be added for life-threatening bleeding or emergency surgery to minimize bleeding risk. Earlier guidance emphasized glucocorticoids/IVIG with immunosuppressants and allowed rituximab for life-threatening hematologic involvement but did not include TPO-RAs. Rituximab has a delayed onset and is not preferred for immediate peri-procedural hemostasis; emergent splenectomy and tranexamic acid monotherapy are not recommended in these guidelines.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 46-year-old man presents with a painless lump on the lateral aspect of his right thigh that he noticed while showering 6 months ago. He reports no trauma, fevers, weight loss, or neurological symptoms. He thinks the size has been stable. On examination, there is a firm, mobile, nontender, subcutaneous mass measuring approximately 4.5 cm in greatest dimension. No overlying skin changes are noted. 

According to the Spanish Group for Research in Sarcomas (GEIS) guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

Answer.Choice_A: Refer to a sarcoma specialty center for dedicated imaging (eg, MRI) and core-needle biopsy planning
Answer.Choice_B: Observe without referral and re-examine in 3 months because there are no other warning features
Answer.Choice_C: Perform office-based marginal excision under local anesthesia
Answer.Choice_D: Obtain plain radiographs and defer further workup for 6 months if unchanged
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 GEIS guideline identifies four warning signs for suspected soft tissue sarcoma: mass larger than a golf ball (>4.3 cm), recent increase in size, deep location, or pain. A 4.5-cm mass meets the size criterion and warrants referral to a sarcoma center for appropriate imaging and core-needle biopsy planning. Earlier guidance used a >5-cm threshold, under which a 4.5-cm painless superficial mass without growth might have been observed. Office excision risks inadequate margins and contaminates planes; plain radiographs and delayed evaluation are insufficient when the size criterion is met.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 37-year-old woman presents after core needle biopsy of a painless enlarging mass in the posterior thigh. MRI of the thigh shows a 9-cm, lobulated, T2-hyperintense mass with prominent myxoid stroma; pathology confirms myxoid liposarcoma. Physical examination is unremarkable. Basic laboratory studies are within normal limits. She is otherwise healthy and is scheduled for definitive local therapy. You plan her initial distant staging.

According to the Spanish Group for Research in Sarcomas (GEIS) guideline issued in 2025, which of the following imaging strategies should be performed for distant staging in this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Whole-body MRI covering neck, chest, abdomen, pelvis, and extremities for distant staging
Answer.Choice_B: Multislice chest CT and abdominal CT, with selective spine MRI to evaluate extrapulmonary metastases
Answer.Choice_C: 18F-FDG PET/CT as the primary whole-body staging study, reserving MRI only for symptomatic sites
Answer.Choice_D: Bone scintigraphy plus contrast-enhanced chest CT and dedicated liver MRI for metastatic evaluation
Answer.Explanation: Myxoid liposarcoma has a predilection for extrapulmonary metastases, including soft tissues and bone marrow, which can be poorly detected by bone scintigraphy and variably avid on FDG PET. The 2025 GEIS guideline recommends whole-body MRI for staging in myxoid liposarcoma; if whole-body MRI is unavailable, thoracoabdominal CT plus spine and pelvic MRI is advised. Earlier guidance emphasized abdominal CT (with possible spine MRI) in addition to routine chest imaging. Therefore, the preferred approach now is whole-body MRI for distant staging. Options C and D are plausible but suboptimal due to PET and bone scan limitations in this subtype.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 32-year-old woman presents with a slowly enlarging, palpable mass in the proximal right thigh noticed 6 months ago. She reports mild, intermittent aching controlled with over-the-counter NSAIDs and no functional limitation. MRI shows a 5.2-cm, well-defined, extraabdominal, intermuscular lesion in the anterior compartment consistent with desmoid-type fibromatosis, abutting but not encasing the femoral neurovascular bundle. Core needle biopsy confirms desmoid tumour with nuclear β-catenin positivity. There is no evidence of bowel involvement, and she does not have features of familial adenomatous polyposis. Neurologic and vascular examinations are normal. She asks about the initial management strategy.

According to the Spanish Group for Research in Sarcomas (GEIS) clinical practice guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

Answer.Choice_A: Active surveillance with periodic MRI and clinical follow-up, with symptomatic management as needed
Answer.Choice_B: Immediate wide local excision aiming for negative margins as the primary treatment
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate systemic therapy with a tyrosine kinase inhibitor (eg, sorafenib) as first-line treatment
Answer.Choice_D: Definitive external-beam radiotherapy as the initial treatment approach
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 GEIS guideline recommends active surveillance as the first approach after diagnosis for most desmoid tumours, reserving active treatment for persistent progression or critical locations. In a stable, extraabdominal lesion without threat to critical structures, observation with interval imaging is preferred. Earlier guidance positioned surgery as the mainstay of curative treatment, with watch-and-wait limited to small, non-growing, asymptomatic extraabdominal tumours. Systemic therapy and radiotherapy are not first-line for this presentation and are generally considered when progression occurs or in anatomically difficult or morbid cases.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 42-year-old man presents with worsening pain and limited shoulder abduction due to a 7-cm desmoid-type fibromatosis involving the right deltoid and pectoral fascia. Six months of active surveillance shows a 30% increase in maximal dimension on MRI with increasing T2 signal and mass effect on the brachial plexus. The multidisciplinary team deems surgery functionally morbid and radiation suboptimal given potential toxicity. He has no significant comorbidities and has not received prior systemic therapy. He requests initiation of medical treatment to control symptoms and tumor progression.

According to the Spanish Group for Research in Sarcomas (GEIS) guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate systemic therapy to initiate now?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate nirogacestat therapy
Answer.Choice_B: Initiate tamoxifen combined with an NSAID
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate sunitinib therapy
Answer.Choice_D: Initiate pembrolizumab therapy
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 GEIS guideline for desmoid tumors recommends nirogacestat and sorafenib as systemic options supported by level I evidence for patients who require medical therapy due to progression or symptoms, with pazopanib also added (II,B). Earlier guidance (2016) emphasized NSAIDs and anti-estrogens (e.g., tamoxifen) as well as imatinib and chemotherapy; while these remain options, they are supported by lower-level evidence compared with nirogacestat or sorafenib. Sunitinib and immune checkpoint inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab) are not recommended in the GEIS guidelines for this indication. Therefore, initiating nirogacestat is the most appropriate choice reflecting the updated recommendations.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 66-year-old man with mantle cell lymphoma (MCL) diagnosed 3 years ago presents with progressive fatigue, night sweats, and enlarging cervical and axillary lymph nodes. At diagnosis, an excisional lymph node biopsy showed cyclin D1–positive MCL with a Ki-67 index of 12% by immunohistochemistry. He was treated with bendamustine-rituximab and achieved a complete remission; he now has symptomatic relapse and is planned to start a Bruton tyrosine kinase inhibitor. According to the European Hematology Association (EHA) Guidelines Committee and European MCL Network guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate next step before initiating new therapy?

Answer.Choice_A: Obtain a repeat tissue biopsy and reassess Ki-67 (and TP53 mutation status if feasible) prior to treatment initiation
Answer.Choice_B: Proceed with treatment without repeating Ki-67, relying on the baseline Ki-67 obtained at initial diagnosis
Answer.Choice_C: Obtain FDG PET–CT and use metabolic activity as a surrogate for proliferation instead of repeating Ki-67
Answer.Choice_D: Perform peripheral blood flow cytometry to estimate proliferation in lieu of a repeat tissue biopsy
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 EHA/European MCL Network guideline recommends that when a new treatment is required in MCL, clinicians should perform a repeat biopsy and reassess Ki-67 and TP53 status whenever possible before initiating therapy. This reflects recognition that proliferation and genomic features can evolve at relapse and may influence prognosis and treatment choice. Earlier guidance (2009) emphasized Ki-67 assessment at primary diagnosis only and did not endorse its use in relapsed disease. PET–CT metabolic activity and peripheral blood flow cytometry are not recommended substitutes for tissue Ki-67 assessment.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 68-year-old woman with stage I left breast cancer is scheduled for adjuvant external beam radiotherapy today. During morning QA, the on-board imager on the linear accelerator was found to be nonfunctional and a vendor service engineer replaced a failed high-voltage board. The department’s policy requires a post-repair equipment performance evaluation (EPE) before resuming clinical use. A Qualified Medical Physicist (QMP) is on site but is occupied with a CT simulation; an experienced medical physics assistant is available and has been trained on the relevant EPE procedures. The QMP can be physically present to supervise but cannot personally perform all test steps before the patient’s time slot. According to the American Association of Physicists in Medicine professional practice guideline issued in 2025, which of the following approaches is recommended for this post-repair EPE?

Answer.Choice_A: Have the trained assistant complete the post-repair EPE under the QMP’s direct on-site supervision before returning the system to clinical use
Answer.Choice_B: Require the QMP to personally complete the post-repair EPE before returning the system to clinical use without delegation
Answer.Choice_C: Allow a dosimetrist to complete the post-repair EPE under general remote supervision by the QMP with next-day result review
Answer.Choice_D: Resume treatments after the service engineer’s verification and schedule the EPE within 72 hours under standard operating procedures
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 AAPM guideline permits annual and post-repair equipment performance evaluations for external beam therapy and associated image guidance systems to be performed either by a QMP or by others under the direct supervision of a QMP. Direct supervision implies the QMP is physically present and immediately available to oversee and direct the activity. Therefore, a trained assistant performing the EPE under the QMP’s direct on-site supervision is acceptable. Earlier guidance (e.g., 2018) required the QMP to personally perform these EPEs. Remote/general supervision (C) is insufficient for post-repair EPEs, and delaying the EPE until after clinical use (D) is not compliant.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 66-year-old woman with stage IB endometrioid endometrial carcinoma is scheduled for her first high–dose-rate (HDR) vaginal cuff brachytherapy fraction at a community radiation oncology center that also performs stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) and stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT). The department is revising its supervision policy for Qualified Medical Physicist (QMP) involvement in the development and delivery of special radiotherapy procedures. The existing policy states that a QMP provides direct supervision for all special procedures. According to the American Association of Physicists in Medicine guideline issued in 2025, which of the following supervision requirements should the department adopt?

Answer.Choice_A: Require a QMP to provide personal or direct supervision for special radiotherapy procedures, as specified by applicable AAPM recommendations
Answer.Choice_B: Require a QMP to provide direct supervision for all special radiotherapy procedures, regardless of procedure-specific recommendations
Answer.Choice_C: Allow general supervision by a QMP for special radiotherapy procedures once written policies and procedures are in place
Answer.Choice_D: Permit delegation of supervision of special radiotherapy procedures to a senior medical dosimetrist if a QMP is available by phone
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 AAPM practice guideline specifies that QMP involvement in the development and delivery of special radiotherapy procedures should be at the personal or direct supervision level, depending on procedure-specific recommendations. This refines earlier guidance that uniformly indicated direct supervision for all special procedures. Thus, the department should require personal or direct supervision as indicated by the applicable AAPM recommendations.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 72-year-old man with metastatic urothelial carcinoma of the bladder presents after radiographic progression on first-line gemcitabine–cisplatin. He completed 6 cycles 6 weeks ago with stable disease, but a restaging CT now shows new pulmonary nodules and enlargement of known pelvic lymph nodes. He has an ECOG performance status of 1. He has no history of autoimmune disease, is not on immunosuppression, and has normal hepatic and renal function. PD-L1 status is unknown, and tumor testing shows no FGFR2/3 alterations. He did not receive maintenance avelumab. According to the Spanish Society of Medical Oncology (SEOM) and the Spanish Group Association of Genitourinary Oncology (SOGUG) clinical practice guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in systemic therapy?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate pembrolizumab monotherapy
Answer.Choice_B: Initiate vinflunine monotherapy
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate docetaxel monotherapy
Answer.Choice_D: Initiate enfortumab vedotin monotherapy
Answer.Explanation: The SEOM/SOGUG 2025 guideline recommends PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors as standard options after progression on first-line platinum-based therapy for metastatic urothelial carcinoma, based on randomized evidence (e.g., KEYNOTE-045) showing an overall survival benefit of pembrolizumab over chemotherapy. Atezolizumab is also listed as an option. Vinflunine (choice B) was recommended in earlier guidance but has been supplanted by immune checkpoint blockade. Docetaxel (choice C) is a plausible chemotherapy alternative but does not confer the survival advantage demonstrated with PD-1 inhibition and is not guideline-preferred in this setting. Enfortumab vedotin (choice D) is typically used after prior platinum and immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy; it is not the recommended immediate option following first-line platinum progression in this guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 68-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents for surgical management of muscle-invasive bladder cancer. Transurethral resection of a bladder tumor revealed high-grade urothelial carcinoma invading the muscularis propria. After receiving neoadjuvant gemcitabine/cisplatin, restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis shows no pelvic or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy and no distant metastases. He is scheduled for radical cystectomy. According to the Spanish Society of Medical Oncology (SEOM) and the Spanish Group Association of Genitourinary Oncology (SOGUG) guideline issued in 2025, which of the following best describes the recommended extent of lymph node dissection at the time of cystectomy?

Answer.Choice_A: Radical cystectomy with standard pelvic lymph node dissection limited to the obturator, external iliac, and internal iliac nodal packets
Answer.Choice_B: Radical cystectomy with extended pelvic lymph node dissection including presacral and common iliac nodes up to the aortic bifurcation
Answer.Choice_C: Radical cystectomy with super-extended lymph node dissection to the inferior mesenteric artery including paraaortic and paracaval nodes
Answer.Choice_D: Radical cystectomy without pelvic lymph node dissection when preoperative imaging shows no enlarged nodes
Answer.Explanation: SEOM/SOGUG 2025 advises against extended lymphadenectomy at cystectomy for muscle-invasive bladder cancer because prospective data have not shown a survival benefit over a standard pelvic template and extended dissection increases morbidity. Therefore, a standard pelvic lymph node dissection (obturator, external and internal iliac) is recommended. The prior approach favored extended dissection to the aortic bifurcation, which is no longer recommended. Super-extended templates and omission of nodal dissection are not advised in clinically node-negative patients.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 68-year-old man is seen 5 weeks after radical cystectomy with extended pelvic lymph node dissection for muscle-invasive urothelial carcinoma of the bladder. He did not receive neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Final pathology shows high-grade urothelial carcinoma, pT3a pN1, with negative surgical margins. He has an ECOG performance status of 0, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR 90 mL/min/1.73 m²), no neuropathy, and no hearing impairment. He asks about additional treatment to reduce recurrence risk.

According to the Spanish Society of Medical Oncology (SEOM) and the Spanish Group Association of Genitourinary Oncology (SOGUG) clinical practice guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy (e.g., gemcitabine–cisplatin) for 4–6 cycles
Answer.Choice_B: Defer systemic therapy and pursue surveillance, as adjuvant chemotherapy is not routinely supported by sufficient evidence
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate adjuvant carboplatin-based chemotherapy (e.g., gemcitabine–carboplatin) to reduce toxicity
Answer.Choice_D: Administer adjuvant pelvic radiotherapy with concurrent chemotherapy to reduce local recurrence
Answer.Explanation: For patients with high-risk pathologic features after cystectomy (pT3/4 and/or pN+) who did not receive neoadjuvant chemotherapy and are eligible for cisplatin, the SEOM–SOGUG 2025 guideline recommends adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy. Earlier guidance (2016) did not endorse routine adjuvant chemotherapy due to insufficient evidence, although it suggested that high-risk patients might derive the greatest benefit. Carboplatin-based regimens lack evidence of benefit in this setting for cisplatin-eligible patients, and pelvic radiotherapy is not recommended as adjuvant therapy to reduce systemic recurrence risk.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 71-year-old man presents with newly diagnosed metastatic urothelial carcinoma of the bladder with multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules and two liver lesions. He has not received prior systemic therapy. He has an ECOG performance status of 1, creatinine clearance of 78 mL/min, normal liver function tests, and no history of autoimmune disease or severe neuropathy. He asks about the best initial systemic treatment. 

According to the Spanish Society of Medical Oncology (SEOM) and the Spanish Group Association of Genitourinary Oncology (SOGUG) guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate first-line regimen for this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Enfortumab vedotin plus pembrolizumab
Answer.Choice_B: Cisplatin plus gemcitabine
Answer.Choice_C: Dose-dense MVAC (methotrexate, vinblastine, doxorubicin, and cisplatin)
Answer.Choice_D: Gemcitabine plus carboplatin
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SEOM–SOGUG guideline, informed by the phase III EV-302/KEYNOTE-A39 trial, designates enfortumab vedotin plus pembrolizumab as a standard-of-care preferred first-line treatment for most patients with metastatic urothelial carcinoma due to significant overall and progression-free survival benefits. Previously, cisplatin-based chemotherapy—most commonly cisplatin plus gemcitabine—was considered the standard first-line option (preferred over MVAC/ddMVAC due to better safety). Dose-dense MVAC is more toxic and not preferred over gemcitabine–cisplatin, and gemcitabine–carboplatin is reserved for cisplatin-ineligible patients; neither is the recommended preferred regimen in this setting.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 34-year-old woman with aggressive relapsing–remitting multiple sclerosis has had three disabling relapses in the past year despite sequential use of natalizumab and ocrelizumab. MRI shows new enhancing periventricular lesions. She is scheduled for autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) using an intermediate-intensity conditioning regimen. Pretransplant evaluation shows normal CBC and chemistry panel; serologic testing for HIV, HBV, and HCV is negative. The transplant team is selecting the serotherapy component of conditioning.

According to the European Society for Blood and Marrow Transplantation (EBMT) guideline issued in 2025 for autologous HSCT in autoimmune diseases, which of the following serotherapy strategies is recommended?

Answer.Choice_A: Use polyclonal anti–thymocyte globulin (ATG) and avoid alemtuzumab in the conditioning regimen
Answer.Choice_B: Include alemtuzumab as the serotherapy agent within the conditioning regimen, with standard infection prophylaxis
Answer.Choice_C: Administer low-dose alemtuzumab in combination with reduced-intensity conditioning to limit toxicity
Answer.Choice_D: Substitute rituximab as the serotherapy agent for T-cell depletion within the conditioning regimen
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 EBMT guideline for autologous HSCT in autoimmune diseases specifically advises against using alemtuzumab as serotherapy in the conditioning regimen due to associations with increased mortality and a higher risk of secondary autoimmune diseases. Earlier guidance (2012) permitted monoclonal antibodies such as alemtuzumab as options based on availability, but this has changed. Therefore, selecting polyclonal ATG and avoiding alemtuzumab aligns with the 2025 recommendation. Including alemtuzumab (B) reflects older practice; dose reduction (C) is not recommended; rituximab (D) is not an established T-cell–depleting serotherapy for this indication.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 42-year-old woman with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis underwent autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) 4 months ago after cyclophosphamide-based conditioning. She has been taking valacyclovir 500 mg twice daily since conditioning. She has had no episodes of herpes reactivation. Laboratory testing today shows a CD4+ T-cell count of 0.15 × 10^9/L. She asks if she can stop the antiviral now. According to the European Society for Blood and Marrow Transplantation guideline issued in 2025 for infection prophylaxis in autologous HSCT for autoimmune diseases, which of the following is the most appropriate antiviral prophylaxis plan?

Answer.Choice_A: Continue valacyclovir 500 mg twice daily until 12 months post-HSCT and reassess; if CD4+ remains <0.2 × 10^9/L at that time, continue until it exceeds that threshold
Answer.Choice_B: Stop valacyclovir 500 mg twice daily now that she is beyond day +100 post-HSCT
Answer.Choice_C: Continue valacyclovir 500 mg twice daily but plan to stop at 6 months post-HSCT if there is no clinical reactivation
Answer.Choice_D: Continue valacyclovir 500 mg twice daily indefinitely without a planned stop date
Answer.Explanation: EBMT 2025 guidelines for autologous HSCT in autoimmune/rheumatic diseases recommend antiviral prophylaxis for all patients from conditioning until 1 year post-transplant and/or until CD4+ count exceeds 0.2 × 10^9/L. At day +120 with a CD4+ count of 0.15, she should continue prophylaxis, with reassessment at 12 months and extension beyond 1 year if CD4+ remains below 0.2. Earlier guidance (2012) recommended herpes prophylaxis during and for at least 100 days post-transplant; stopping at day +100 (option B) is no longer sufficient. Stopping at 6 months (option C) under-treats many patients with prolonged immunosuppression, and indefinite prophylaxis (option D) is not recommended.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 45-year-old man with aggressive relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis underwent autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation 120 days ago. Conditioning included cyclophosphamide and antithymocyte globulin. Pretransplant serology was positive for Toxoplasma gondii IgG. He has been taking trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) three times weekly since conditioning started and has tolerated it well. He is clinically well with an absolute neutrophil count of 2.1 × 10^9/L and a CD4+ T-cell count of 0.14 × 10^9/L. He asks when he can stop infection prophylaxis.

According to the European Society for Blood and Marrow Transplantation (EBMT) guideline issued in 2025 for autologous HSCT in autoimmune/rheumatic diseases, which of the following prophylaxis schedules is recommended?

Answer.Choice_A: Continue TMP-SMX from conditioning through 6 months post-transplant and, if CD4+ count remains ≤0.2 × 10^9/L, continue until it exceeds that threshold; for toxoplasma seropositivity, complete a 6-month course
Answer.Choice_B: Discontinue TMP-SMX at day +100 post-transplant regardless of CD4+ count; for toxoplasma seropositivity, limit prophylaxis to at least 100 days
Answer.Choice_C: Discontinue TMP-SMX once neutrophil engraftment is achieved (ANC >0.5 × 10^9/L) and mucositis resolves, with no extension based on CD4+ count or serostatus
Answer.Choice_D: Continue TMP-SMX for 12 months post-transplant irrespective of CD4+ T-cell recovery or toxoplasma serostatus
Answer.Explanation: EBMT 2025 guidance for autologous HSCT in autoimmune/rheumatic diseases recommends anti-Pneumocystis (and anti-toxoplasma) prophylaxis starting at conditioning and continuing until 6 months post-transplant and/or until CD4+ count exceeds 0.2 × 10^9/L; toxoplasma-seropositive patients should receive TMP-SMX for 6 months. This updates earlier guidance that used a fixed minimum of ~100 days post-transplant.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 45-year-old woman with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis underwent autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) 60 days ago for refractory disease. She is clinically stable with improving functional status, normal blood counts, and no infectious complications. She lives 200 miles from the transplant center and asks whether she can now transition her follow-up to her local rheumatologist and reduce the frequency of visits. 

According to the European Society for Blood and Marrow Transplantation (EBMT) guideline issued in 2025, which of the following follow-up plans is most appropriate?

Answer.Choice_A: Continue direct routine care under the HSCT specialist through day 100; thereafter implement standardized follow-up at least every 6 months for the first 2 years and annually if medically stable, with combined HSCT–organ specialist care and a joint annual review
Answer.Choice_B: Arrange quarterly visits during the first year and biannual consultations thereafter, ideally with dual review by hematology and the disease specialist
Answer.Choice_C: Transition now to rheumatology-led care with annual reviews only, requesting HSCT input as needed if new problems arise
Answer.Choice_D: Schedule weekly HSCT clinic visits for the first 6 months, then discharge to primary care with no routine specialty follow-up once clinically stable
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 EBMT guidance for autologous HSCT in autoimmune/rheumatic diseases recommends careful, standardized follow-up for the first 100 days under direct HSCT specialist care, then at least every 6 months for the first 2 years and annually thereafter if stable; ongoing combined care with joint annual review is advised. In contrast, earlier EBMT guidance (2015) emphasized quarterly visits in year 1 and biannual follow-up thereafter, ideally with dual review, without specifying the first-100-day HSCT specialist oversight or the 2-year semiannual cadence. Thus, at day 60 post-transplant, she should remain under HSCT specialist care until at least day 100 and then follow the semiannual/annual schedule with combined care.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 42-year-old woman with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis refractory to cyclophosphamide and mycophenolate mofetil has completed stem cell mobilization and collection and is scheduled to start conditioning next week for autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT). You are designing her post-transplant immunological monitoring plan (including lymphocyte subsets, immunoglobulin levels, and disease-specific autoantibodies). According to the European Society for Blood and Marrow Transplantation (EBMT) guideline issued in 2025 for HSCT in autoimmune/rheumatic diseases, which monitoring schedule is recommended?

Answer.Choice_A: Baseline assessments before mobilization and before conditioning; then at 3, 6, and 12 months in year 1; every 6 months in year 2; and annually through year 5
Answer.Choice_B: Baseline assessment before mobilization and before SCT; then quarterly during the first year; and every 6 months thereafter without a defined stop
Answer.Choice_C: Baseline assessment before conditioning only; then at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months in year 1; and annually thereafter indefinitely
Answer.Choice_D: Baseline assessment before mobilization only; then at 6 and 12 months in year 1; every 6 months during years 2–5; and discontinue routine monitoring thereafter
Answer.Explanation: EBMT 2025 guidance for autologous HSCT in autoimmune/rheumatic diseases specifies two baseline time points—before mobilization and before conditioning—and standardized intervals of immune monitoring at 3, 6, and 12 months in the first year, every 6 months in the second year, and then annually up to 5 years. Earlier EBMT guidance (2015) recommended a single baseline before mobilization/SCT with quarterly monitoring in the first year and biannual monitoring indefinitely.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 45-year-old Korean woman presents for an annual health examination. She reports gaining 6 kg over the last 3 years. She is 160 cm tall and weighs 62 kg (BMI 24.2 kg/m2). Blood pressure is 148/92 mm Hg. Waist circumference is 87 cm. Laboratory studies show HbA1c 7.6%, triglycerides 260 mg/dL, HDL cholesterol 39 mg/dL, ALT 72 U/L; thyroid-stimulating hormone is normal. Abdominal ultrasonography demonstrates hepatic steatosis without fibrosis. She asks whether she meets criteria for obesity.

According to the Korean Society for the Study of Obesity guideline issued in 2024, which of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic approach for this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Use the elevated waist circumference to confirm excess adiposity in addition to BMI and diagnose clinical obesity because of diabetes and hepatic steatosis
Answer.Choice_B: Refrain from diagnosing obesity because BMI is <25 kg/m2; classify her as non-obese and address comorbidities separately
Answer.Choice_C: Diagnose obesity based solely on waist circumference, disregarding BMI and comorbidities
Answer.Choice_D: Obtain dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry to measure body fat percentage and defer any obesity diagnosis until BMI reaches ≥25 kg/m2
Answer.Explanation: The 2024 Korean Society for the Study of Obesity guideline adopts a two-step process: confirm excess adiposity using at least one anthropometric measure or direct body fat assessment in addition to BMI, then evaluate for clinical obesity—excess adiposity with organ dysfunction, metabolic abnormalities, or functional impairments—regardless of BMI. This patient’s elevated waist circumference confirms excess adiposity, and her diabetes and hepatic steatosis indicate clinical obesity. The 2023 guideline relied on BMI thresholds alone (≥25 kg/m2) and would not diagnose obesity in this case.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 44-year-old Korean man presents for a routine health evaluation. He has gained 6 kg over the past 2 years. He takes no medications. Family history is notable for type 2 diabetes in his father. On examination, blood pressure is 138/86 mm Hg. Height is 170 cm and weight is 75.5 kg (BMI 26.1 kg/m²). Waist circumference has not yet been measured. Laboratory testing shows fasting glucose 104 mg/dL, HbA1c 6.0%, triglycerides 220 mg/dL, HDL cholesterol 38 mg/dL, and ALT 52 U/L. He asks whether he meets criteria for obesity and what further diagnostic steps are needed.

According to the Korean Society for the Study of Obesity guideline issued in 2024, which of the following diagnostic approaches is recommended?

Answer.Choice_A: Confirm excess adiposity by measuring an anthropometric criterion (e.g., waist circumference) or a direct body fat measure (e.g., bioimpedance or DXA) in addition to BMI, then assess for clinical obesity by evaluating for organ dysfunction or metabolic/functional abnormalities
Answer.Choice_B: Diagnose obesity based on BMI ≥25 kg/m² and record waist circumference only to refine cardiometabolic risk, without requiring a confirmatory adiposity test
Answer.Choice_C: Diagnose obesity only when both BMI ≥25 kg/m² and waist circumference ≥90 cm are present; otherwise classify as overweight and defer additional evaluation
Answer.Choice_D: Use abdominal CT to quantify visceral fat as the primary confirmatory test and omit anthropometric measures such as waist circumference from the diagnostic process
Answer.Explanation: The 2024 Korean Society for the Study of Obesity guideline adopts a two-step diagnostic process: use BMI as an initial screen and require confirmation of excess adiposity with at least one anthropometric criterion (e.g., waist circumference) or a direct body fat assessment; subsequently evaluate for clinical obesity, defined as excess adiposity with organ dysfunction, metabolic abnormalities, or functional impairments. Earlier guidance prioritized BMI thresholds and recommended waist circumference for risk stratification but did not require confirmatory adiposity measures or include the clinical obesity construct. Options C and D are not recommended approaches in the guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 38-year-old man presents with a 9-cm expanding annular erythematous rash on his left thigh 10 days after a hiking trip in a wooded area. He reports mild fatigue but denies fever, arthralgias, facial weakness, palpitations, or dyspnea. Vital signs are normal. Examination shows a classic erythema migrans lesion without regional lymphadenopathy. He recalls significant photosensitivity while taking doxycycline for acne in the past and gastrointestinal upset with amoxicillin; he asks whether a penicillin tablet would work as well as doxycycline or amoxicillin for this rash.

According to the German Dermatology Society (Deutsche Dermatologische Gesellschaft, DDG) clinical practice guideline issued in 2025, which of the following antibiotic regimens is recommended as an equally effective alternative to doxycycline and amoxicillin for early cutaneous Lyme borreliosis, including appropriate dosing?

Answer.Choice_A: Phenoxymethylpenicillin (penicillin V) 1 million IU orally three times daily for 14 days
Answer.Choice_B: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 10 days
Answer.Choice_C: Azithromycin 500 mg orally once daily for 5 days
Answer.Choice_D: Amoxicillin–clavulanate 875/125 mg orally twice daily for 14 days
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 DDG guideline, citing a Cochrane network meta-analysis, states that oral penicillin V is just as effective as amoxicillin and doxycycline for early cutaneous Lyme borreliosis and provides specific dosing: adults 1 million IU three times daily for 14 days (children 100,000 IU/kg/day for 14 days). Earlier DDG guidance (2017) characterized penicillin V as controversial and did not endorse it with dosing or as equivalent to doxycycline/amoxicillin. Doxycycline remains effective but is not the asked-for alternative; macrolides such as azithromycin are less preferred due to lower efficacy, and amoxicillin–clavulanate is unnecessary for erythema migrans.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A public health dentist in a rural West African district hospital is updating a two-day training for community health and social care workers in a noma‑endemic region. The curriculum previously covered recognition of acute noma, referral pathways, nutrition support, and long-term functional sequelae. A 9-year-old girl with healed noma and severe right-sided facial scarring was recently seen in clinic; her family reports she has stopped attending school due to teasing and that neighbors avoid interacting with them. Physical examination shows significant midfacial tissue loss with oral incompetence but no active infection. The dentist wants the updated course to align with the FDI World Dental Federation guidance issued in 2026 for professionals working in noma‑endemic areas. Which of the following training elements should be incorporated?

Answer.Choice_A: Include structured mental health counselling approaches aimed at reducing stigma and facilitating social reintegration for survivors and their families
Answer.Choice_B: Limit content to acknowledging the lifelong impact of noma without adding specific counselling strategies or reintegration components
Answer.Choice_C: Center the curriculum on surgical reconstruction and facial prosthesis referral pathways as the primary means to achieve social reintegration
Answer.Choice_D: Emphasize only acute-phase medical-surgical management (eg, antibiotics, debridement) while deferring community follow-up to external organizations
Answer.Explanation: According to the FDI World Dental Federation guideline issued in 2026, training for health and social care professionals in noma-endemic areas should recognize the lifelong impact of noma and explicitly include the importance of mental health counselling to help remove stigma and enhance reintegration of survivors and their families. Earlier guidance emphasized recognizing lifelong impact without specifying counselling components. Focusing solely on surgical pathways or acute-phase care does not address the psychosocial needs central to community reintegration.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 32-year-old public health dentist is updating a training curriculum for community health workers in a rural district with high rates of malnutrition and suspected noma. Recent field reports describe many children presenting with gum erythema and bleeding without ulceration, and occasional cases with painful necrotising gingival lesions and facial swelling. The dentist wants the curriculum to reflect guideline-based education on early recognition of noma stages. According to the FDI World Dental Federation guideline issued in 2026, which training content should be included regarding the reversible stages, in addition to instruction on the irreversible stages (gangrenous, scarring, and sequelae)?

Answer.Choice_A: Include recognition of simple gingivitis, necrotising ulcerative gingivitis, and oedema as reversible stages, alongside instruction on gangrenous, scarring, and sequelae as irreversible stages
Answer.Choice_B: Include recognition of necrotising ulcerative gingivitis and oedema only as reversible stages, alongside instruction on gangrenous, scarring, and sequelae as irreversible stages
Answer.Choice_C: Include recognition of aphthous stomatitis, necrotising ulcerative gingivitis, and oedema as reversible stages, alongside instruction on gangrenous, scarring, and sequelae as irreversible stages
Answer.Choice_D: Include recognition of periodontitis, necrotising ulcerative gingivitis, and oedema as reversible stages, alongside instruction on gangrenous, scarring, and sequelae as irreversible stages
Answer.Explanation: The 2026 FDI World Dental Federation guideline broadens education content for noma-endemic areas to include recognition of simple gingivitis in the reversible stages, in addition to necrotising ulcerative gingivitis and oedema. Earlier guidance focused on necrotising ulcerative gingivitis and oedema without explicitly including simple gingivitis. Aphthous stomatitis and periodontitis are not listed as reversible noma stages in the guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 26-year-old Korean woman presents for a routine health examination. She has a history of polycystic ovary syndrome and reports that her mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus. She is not pregnant. Her BMI is 24 kg/m2, blood pressure is 112/70 mm Hg, and physical examination is unremarkable. She asks whether she should be screened for diabetes.

According to the Korean Diabetes Association guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate diabetes screening now using fasting plasma glucose, hemoglobin A1C, or a 2-hour plasma glucose during a 75-g oral glucose tolerance test
Answer.Choice_B: Defer diabetes screening until she is older than 30 years, then perform annual screening
Answer.Choice_C: Defer screening unless her BMI is ≥25 kg/m2; PCOS and family history alone are insufficient indications
Answer.Choice_D: Recommend lifestyle modification now and perform diabetes screening in 3 years if risk factors persist
Answer.Explanation: Per the Korean Diabetes Association 2025 guideline, all Korean adults aged ≥19 years with one or more diabetes risk factors should be screened for diabetes. This 26-year-old has at least two risk factors (polycystic ovary syndrome and a first-degree relative with type 2 diabetes), so screening should be performed now using standard tests (fasting plasma glucose, A1C, or 2-hour plasma glucose during a 75-g OGTT). Earlier guidance (e.g., 2021) recommended screening adults >30 years with risk factors, which would have deferred screening in this patient. Options C and D are not consistent with the 2025 recommendation and would inappropriately delay screening.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 62-year-old woman presents with progressive dyspnea and pelvic pain 3 months after imaging revealed multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules and peritoneal implants. Biopsy confirms metastatic endometrioid endometrial carcinoma. Immunohistochemistry shows intact MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, and PMS2 (pMMR); HER2 testing is negative. She has not received prior systemic therapy for advanced disease. ECOG performance status is 1; renal and hepatic function are within normal limits. She asks about the recommended first-line systemic regimen.

According to the SEOM–GEICO guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

Answer.Choice_A: Carboplatin–paclitaxel plus pembrolizumab, followed by pembrolizumab maintenance for up to 2 years
Answer.Choice_B: Carboplatin–paclitaxel alone for 6 cycles without immunotherapy
Answer.Choice_C: Pembrolizumab plus lenvatinib without chemotherapy
Answer.Choice_D: Carboplatin–paclitaxel plus bevacizumab, followed by bevacizumab maintenance
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SEOM–GEICO guideline designates chemo-immunotherapy with carboplatin–paclitaxel plus a PD-1 inhibitor (pembrolizumab or dostarlimab), followed by maintenance immunotherapy (2 years with pembrolizumab or 3 years with dostarlimab), as the standard first-line regimen for advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer in both dMMR and pMMR populations, reflecting phase III trials (NRG-GY018 and RUBY). The previous standard was carboplatin–paclitaxel alone (Choice B). Pembrolizumab–lenvatinib (Choice C) is indicated after prior platinum therapy in pMMR disease, not as initial therapy. The addition of bevacizumab to carboplatin–paclitaxel with bevacizumab maintenance (Choice D) is not the recommended standard for first-line treatment in this setting.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with vaginal bleeding. She undergoes total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and sentinel lymph node mapping for endometrial cancer. Final pathology shows a 4.2-cm FIGO grade 3 endometrioid adenocarcinoma with 60% myometrial invasion, no lymphovascular space invasion, negative cervix, and negative sentinel nodes. Molecular testing identifies a pathogenic POLE exonuclease domain mutation; p53 is wild-type and mismatch repair proteins are intact. The oncology team plans adjuvant therapy and asks how best to assign stage for use in multidisciplinary decision-making.

According to the Spanish Society of Medical Oncology (SEOM) and the Spanish Gynecological Cancer Research Group (GEICO) guideline issued in 2025, which of the following staging approaches is recommended?

Answer.Choice_A: Use the FIGO 2023 endometrial cancer staging system, integrating molecular classification (eg, POLE-mutated, MMR-deficient, p53-abnormal) with anatomic factors to inform risk-based management
Answer.Choice_B: Use the FIGO 2009 endometrial cancer staging system based solely on anatomic extent (eg, depth of myometrial invasion and cervical stromal involvement) without molecular integration
Answer.Choice_C: Use the AJCC/UICC TNM 8th edition anatomical staging for uterine corpus carcinomas without incorporating molecular modifiers
Answer.Choice_D: Use the ESGO/ESTRO/ESP 2021 molecular risk groups as the primary staging schema in place of FIGO staging
Answer.Explanation: SEOM-GEICO 2025 recommends adopting the FIGO 2023 endometrial cancer staging update, which incorporates molecular factors and emphasizes risk classification to improve prognostic precision and guide therapy, replacing the previously used FIGO 2009 anatomic-only staging. Option B reflects the earlier anatomic-only FIGO 2009 approach. Options C and D are plausible frameworks but are not the SEOM-GEICO 2025-recommended staging system for endometrial cancer.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 66-year-old man is in the surgical ICU after a difficult airway course for composite resection of an oral cavity tumor with bilateral neck dissection. He previously received chemoradiation to the neck and has limited mouth opening. Intubation required awake fiberoptic techniques, and significant supraglottic edema was noted. Before extubation today, a cuff-leak test showed minimal leak. He was extubated over an airway exchange catheter (AEC) and is now stable on humidified oxygen without distress, but there is persistent neck swelling and the team anticipates peak edema over the next several hours. According to the All India Difficult Airway Association guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate duration to maintain the AEC in situ in this high-risk scenario?

Answer.Choice_A: Maintain the AEC for up to 12 hours, then remove if the airway remains stable
Answer.Choice_B: Maintain the AEC for up to 48 hours to bridge the period of maximal edema
Answer.Choice_C: Remove the AEC immediately now that the patient is breathing spontaneously
Answer.Choice_D: Maintain the AEC for approximately 24 hours before reassessment and removal
Answer.Explanation: In the 2025 All India Difficult Airway Association guidance, an airway exchange catheter may be maintained for 2 hours after extubation and, in extreme situations with high risk of airway collapse, for up to 12 hours. This patient’s radiated neck, limited mouth opening, difficult intubation, minimal cuff leak, and expected peak edema represent an extreme-risk scenario, supporting retention up to 12 hours. Earlier guidance (2016) allowed retention for up to 2 days, but the newer recommendation shortens the maximum suggested dwell time to 12 hours. Options B and D exceed this cap, and immediate removal (C) is not advised in an at-risk extubation.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 62-year-old woman undergoes total thyroidectomy under general anesthesia with rocuronium. At the end of the case, sevoflurane is discontinued and neostigmine with glycopyrrolate is administered. She is awake, follows commands, and has sustained handgrip. Qualitative peripheral nerve stimulation shows four twitches with no obvious fade. A calibrated quantitative neuromuscular monitor (acceleromyography) is available in the operating room. According to the All India Difficult Airway Association guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is recommended before extubation?

Answer.Choice_A: Confirm a train-of-four ratio greater than 0.9 using quantitative neuromuscular monitoring prior to extubation, given device availability
Answer.Choice_B: Proceed to extubation after antagonizing residual block and assessing clinical signs of recovery without requiring quantitative TOF measurements
Answer.Choice_C: Rely on qualitative train-of-four assessment (four twitches with no visible fade) without quantitative confirmation, then extubate
Answer.Choice_D: If sugammadex has been administered, omit neuromuscular monitoring and extubate when the patient follows commands
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 All India Difficult Airway Association guideline recommends performing quantitative neuromuscular monitoring and confirming a TOF ratio >0.9 before extubation when equipment is available. Earlier guidance emphasized antagonizing residual blockade and assessing recovery without specifying a quantitative threshold. Options C and D are plausible practices but are not recommended by this guideline update.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 46-year-old man is scheduled for urgent laparoscopic appendectomy for acute appendicitis. He has hypertension and a BMI of 28 kg/m2. He has been fasting for 10 hours and is hemodynamically stable. Airway evaluation shows inter-incisor distance of 4.5 cm, Mallampati class II, thyromental distance of 7 cm, normal neck extension, and no facial hair. A standard Macintosh direct laryngoscope and a videolaryngoscope (Macintosh and hyperangulated blades) are immediately available. The consultant anesthesiologist is experienced with direct laryngoscopy and trained in videolaryngoscopy. According to the All India Difficult Airway Association guideline issued in 2025, which of the following device strategies should be used for the first intubation attempt to maximize first-pass success?

Answer.Choice_A: Use videolaryngoscopy for the first intubation attempt, with a stylet or bougie available as needed
Answer.Choice_B: Use direct laryngoscopy for the first attempt based on operator experience, reserving videolaryngoscopy for rescue or anticipated difficulty
Answer.Choice_C: Use direct laryngoscopy with routine cricoid pressure for all intubations; switch to videolaryngoscopy only after two failed attempts
Answer.Choice_D: Use flexible fiberoptic bronchoscopy as the first-line device for elective and routine surgical airways to minimize trauma
Answer.Explanation: The All India Difficult Airway Association (AIDAA) 2025 guideline recommends using videolaryngoscopy as the default tool for laryngoscopy, when feasible, to improve first-pass intubation success, and encourages the use of adjuncts such as stylets and bougies as appropriate. Earlier guidance (2016) allowed either direct or video laryngoscopy based on operator experience and comfort, with emphasis on access and training. In a routine airway with equipment and trained personnel available, selecting videolaryngoscopy for the initial attempt best aligns with the 2025 recommendation and evidence of higher first-pass success, especially in difficult airways.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 62-year-old woman living in a remote community in regional Australia presents to her general practitioner with a 3-week history of a rapidly growing, irregularly pigmented lesion on her upper back and scattered erythematous scaly plaques on her elbows. On examination via the GP’s clinic, the lesion is 8 mm with variegated color and irregular borders; the plaques are consistent with chronic plaque psoriasis. The GP requests urgent dermatology input to triage the pigmented lesion and advice on psoriasis management. The local dermatology service is updating its teledermatology standard operating procedures to align with the Australasian College of Dermatologists teledermatology guideline issued in 2025 for consultations, clinician-to-clinician advice, triage, and follow-up. Which of the following lists best reflects the modalities explicitly encompassed by the guideline’s scope?

Answer.Choice_A: Store-and-forward (SAF), real-time videoconsultation (RTVC), real-time telephone consultation (RTTC), and hybrid use of these modalities
Answer.Choice_B: Store-and-forward (SAF), real-time videoconsultation (RTVC), and hybrid use of these modalities without telephone consultations
Answer.Choice_C: Real-time telephone consultation (RTTC) only, excluding video or store-and-forward
Answer.Choice_D: Real-time videoconsultation (RTVC) only, excluding store-and-forward or telephone
Answer.Explanation: According to the Australasian College of Dermatologists teledermatology guideline issued in 2025, the scope explicitly includes SAF, RTVC, RTTC, and hybrid use of these modalities for consultations, triage, advice, and follow-up. The 2020 guideline listed SAF, RTVC, and hybrid modalities but did not include real-time telephone consultation.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 42-year-old man schedules a same-day audio-only telehealth visit with a dermatologist for rapidly spreading pruritic vesicles after yard work. The dermatologist plans to use a clinic speakerphone with a dermatology registrar present in the room. At the start of the call, the patient mentions that his partner is nearby but will not be speaking. The dermatologist intends to discuss diagnosis and treatment options.

According to the Australasian College of Dermatologists clinical guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate next step at the beginning of this real-time teleconsultation?
