Answer.Choice_A: Make your identity known, confirm the patient’s identity, and have both parties identify all persons attending or who may be able to hear the consultation; explain the process before proceeding.
Answer.Choice_B: Confirm the patient’s identity and proceed; identification of other individuals is only required for video consultations where attendees are on screen.
Answer.Choice_C: Begin the call and record the consultation for the medical record without additional consent, as participation in the telehealth visit implies consent to recording.
Answer.Choice_D: Require conversion to a video consultation so that all participants can be on screen; if video is not possible, defer to an in-person visit.
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 Australasian College of Dermatologists guideline extends consent and identification requirements to all real-time teleconsultations, including telephone. At the start of the consultation, the clinician should make their identity known, confirm the patient’s identity, explain the process, and ensure that all persons attending or who may be able to hear the consultation on either side are identified. Option B reflects earlier guidance limited to video (RTVC) and only on-screen attendees. Recording without explicit consent (C) is not recommended, and insisting on conversion to video or deferral (D) is unnecessary.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 74-year-old man living in a rural community is scheduled for follow-up teledermatology visits to monitor actinic keratoses and to review images shared from his local nurse. The dermatologist practices in Australia and will decide which videoconferencing platform the clinic will adopt for live consultations and screen-sharing of dermoscopic images. The region’s internet bandwidth is variable. The clinic wants to align its minimum platform specifications with professional guidance.

According to the Australasian College of Dermatologists teledermatology guideline issued in 2025, which set of platform requirements should be specified when the clinician controls the choice of videoconference platform?

Answer.Choice_A: Require non-proprietary, standards-based encryption of authentication, video, and audio; minimum 25 frames per second video; minimum 16 kilobits per second audio; and content sharing capability
Answer.Choice_B: Require standards-based operation (H.323/SIP/WebRTC) or interoperability with these, standards-based codecs, automatic bandwidth adaptation, non-proprietary standards-based encryption, minimum 25 frames per second video, minimum 16 kilobits per second audio, and content sharing capability
Answer.Choice_C: Require end-to-end proprietary encryption as acceptable, minimum 30 frames per second video, minimum 64 kilobits per second audio; content sharing optional
Answer.Choice_D: Require standards-based operation and automatic bandwidth adaptation, but accept minimum 20 frames per second video and minimum 12 kilobits per second audio without content sharing
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 Australasian College of Dermatologists guideline specifies that, when the clinician chooses the videoconference platform, it should provide non-proprietary, standards-based encryption for authentication/video/audio, support at least 25 fps video and a 16 kbps audio channel, and allow content sharing. Earlier guidance (2020) additionally required standards-based operation/interoperability (H.323/SIP/WebRTC) and automatic bandwidth adaptation, which are no longer stipulated. Options C and D either relax required performance/content sharing or accept proprietary encryption, which are not recommended.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 32-year-old woman is undergoing total-body photography for dysplastic nevi surveillance in a dermatology clinic in Sydney. The image set includes standardized full-body and close-up genital photographs. The practice plans to move its image archive to a commercial cloud platform and asks you to specify the storage requirement for these highly sensitive images. They will comply with Australian data residency and seek formal security review. According to the Australasian College of Dermatologists practice guideline issued in 2025, which of the following storage arrangements is recommended?

Answer.Choice_A: Use a well-known cloud provider with strong security protocols; ensure data are stored only within Australia; and complete the Australian Signals Directorate–prescribed risk assessment before deployment.
Answer.Choice_B: Use a cloud service from the Australian Signals Directorate Certified Cloud Services List (CCSL); ensure data are stored only within Australia; and complete the Australian Signals Directorate–prescribed risk assessment before deployment.
Answer.Choice_C: Use any reputable global cloud provider offering end-to-end encryption; allow multi-region replication (including overseas) for redundancy; and forgo a formal risk assessment given strong encryption.
Answer.Choice_D: Avoid cloud services entirely by keeping images on a clinic workstation or local server; disable any off-site backups; and omit a separate risk assessment because access is restricted to the clinic network.
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 Australasian College of Dermatologists guidance advises that highly sensitive images (e.g., nude photographs) may be stored with well-known cloud providers that have strong security controls, while maintaining Australian data residency and completing the Australian Signals Directorate–prescribed risk assessment. Earlier guidance specified using providers from the ASD Certified Cloud Services List (CCSL), which is no longer the recommended criterion. Options C and D conflict with the guideline by permitting overseas storage without mandated risk assessment (C) or avoiding cloud with no formal risk assessment and inadequate resilience (D).
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 32-year-old man is newly diagnosed with HIV-1 infection during routine screening. He has no prior antiretroviral therapy exposure. He takes no medications, including acid-suppressing agents, and has no history of psychiatric illness. Physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show CD4 count 420 cells/μL and HIV RNA 48,000 copies/mL. Genotypic resistance testing shows wild-type virus without major NRTI, NNRTI, PI, or INSTI mutations. Hepatitis B surface antigen is negative, hepatitis B surface antibody is positive, and estimated GFR is 100 mL/min. He prefers a once-daily oral regimen. According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, which of the following NNRTI-based initial regimens is recommended for this ART-naïve adult?

Answer.Choice_A: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/lamivudine/doravirine once daily
Answer.Choice_B: Tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine/rilpivirine with food and without concomitant acid-suppressing agents
Answer.Choice_C: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine/efavirenz once daily
Answer.Choice_D: Abacavir/lamivudine plus rilpivirine with food
Answer.Explanation: EACS 2025 recommends doravirine-based NNRTI regimens for initial therapy (e.g., TDF/3TC/doravirine or TAF/FTC + doravirine) and lists rilpivirine-based regimens as alternatives with specific restrictions (CD4 >200 cells/μL, HIV RNA <100,000 copies/mL, taken with food, and avoidance of acid-suppressing agents). This patient meets criteria for either approach, but the recommended NNRTI option per 2026 EACS is doravirine-based therapy. Rilpivirine-based therapy (B) reflects earlier recommended options that now appear as alternatives. Efavirenz-based therapy (C) and nonstandard combinations such as abacavir/lamivudine plus rilpivirine (D) are not recommended first-line choices in this guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 31-year-old man is establishing care after a new diagnosis of HIV infection. He has no significant medical history and takes no medications. Physical examination is unremarkable. Baseline laboratory testing shows HIV-1 RNA 92,000 copies/mL and CD4 count 430/µL. Genotypic resistance testing reveals no clinically significant mutations. Hepatitis B surface antigen is negative, and hepatitis C antibody is negative. Serum creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73 m²), and liver enzymes are normal. He prefers a once-daily single-tablet regimen to simplify adherence. According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate initial antiretroviral regimen?

Answer.Choice_A: Bictegravir/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine once daily
Answer.Choice_B: Elvitegravir/cobicistat/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine once daily
Answer.Choice_C: Efavirenz/tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine once daily
Answer.Choice_D: Rilpivirine/tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine once daily
Answer.Explanation: EACS 2025 recommends initial ART centered on bictegravir- or dolutegravir-based options (e.g., BIC/TAF/FTC; TAF/FTC or TDF/XTC + DTG; or XTC + DTG), and no longer lists elvitegravir/cobicistat-based single-tablet regimens as recommended first-line choices. Thus BIC/TAF/FTC is an appropriate once-daily single-tablet regimen for this ART-naïve patient without comorbidities or resistance. EVG/c FDCs (e.g., TAF/FTC/EVG/c) were previously listed among recommended initial regimens (e.g., in 2018) but have since been removed. Efavirenz- and rilpivirine-based single-tablets are plausible alternatives in selected settings but are not among the recommended first-line options in the 2026 EACS guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 32-year-old man is newly diagnosed with HIV-1 infection and has not previously received antiretroviral therapy. He has no significant medical history and takes no medications. Baseline labs show HIV-1 RNA 82,000 copies/mL and CD4 count 420/µL; creatinine and liver enzymes are within reference ranges. HBsAg is negative. Baseline resistance testing shows no clinically significant resistance mutations. He is concerned about potential weight gain and asks to start a boosted protease inhibitor–based regimen rather than an integrase inhibitor–based regimen. According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is an appropriate protease inhibitor–based option for initial therapy?

Answer.Choice_A: Tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine/darunavir/cobicistat once daily with food
Answer.Choice_B: Tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine plus atazanavir/cobicistat once daily with food
Answer.Choice_C: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine plus lopinavir/ritonavir twice daily
Answer.Choice_D: Abacavir/lamivudine plus unboosted atazanavir once daily
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 European AIDS Clinical Society guideline lists darunavir-based regimens as the protease inhibitor–based alternatives for initial therapy (TAF/FTC or TDF/XTC + DRV/c or DRV/r, or the single-tablet TAF/FTC/DRV/c). Atazanavir-based boosted regimens, previously included as alternatives, were removed. Therefore, TAF/FTC/DRV/c is an appropriate PI-based initial regimen in an ART-naive adult without contraindications. An atazanavir/c regimen (B) reflects earlier recommendations but is no longer included. Lopinavir/r-based regimens (C) are not recommended for initial therapy. Unboosted atazanavir (D) is not recommended and abacavir use would require HLA-B*57:01 testing before initiation.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 29-year-old woman with HIV presents at 36 weeks’ gestation to discuss infant feeding. She has been on tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine plus dolutegravir since early pregnancy, reports excellent adherence, and plans to continue antiretroviral therapy postpartum. Her HIV-1 RNA has been undetectable (<50 copies/mL) on monthly checks for the past 8 months. She lives in an urban European setting with reliable access to laboratory testing and follow-up. She wishes to breastfeed for cultural reasons but wants to minimize transmission risk and asks for guidance. According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate management approach?

Answer.Choice_A: Offer the option of supported breastfeeding using shared decision-making, with counseling on the very low but non-zero transmission risk and ongoing maternal viral load monitoring
Answer.Choice_B: Advise against breastfeeding and recommend formula feeding; if she chooses to breastfeed anyway, increase maternal and infant clinical and virological monitoring
Answer.Choice_C: Encourage breastfeeding without additional viral load monitoring because sustained viral suppression eliminates transmission risk
Answer.Choice_D: Recommend mixed feeding (alternating breast milk and formula) to reduce exposure and discontinue maternal viral load monitoring unless symptoms arise
Answer.Explanation: EACS 2025 endorses shared decision-making and permits supported breastfeeding for individuals on effective ART who are fully adherent, virally suppressed, and have access to regular viral load monitoring, with counseling about the very low but non-zero risk of transmission. Earlier guidance advised against breastfeeding, recommending enhanced monitoring only if breastfeeding occurred. Options C and D are inconsistent with EACS 2026: ongoing viral load monitoring remains necessary, and mixed feeding is not used to mitigate risk.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 29-year-old woman at 18 weeks’ gestation is newly diagnosed with HIV-1 infection. She is ART-naïve. HIV-1 RNA is 95,000 copies/mL and CD4 count is 420/µL. Hepatitis B surface antigen is negative. Serum creatinine is 0.6 mg/dL with normal estimated GFR. She prefers a once-daily, single-tablet regimen to optimize adherence. According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate initial antiretroviral regimen?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate once-daily tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine/bictegravir fixed-dose combination
Answer.Choice_B: Initiate tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine plus dolutegravir and avoid tenofovir alafenamide–containing regimens during pregnancy due to limited experience
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate once-daily elvitegravir/cobicistat/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine fixed-dose combination
Answer.Choice_D: Initiate once-daily rilpivirine/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine fixed-dose combination
Answer.Explanation: EACS 2025 recommends TAF/FTC/BIC once daily as a preferred initial regimen for ART-naïve pregnant women, expanding on earlier options (e.g., TDF/XTC or TAF/FTC + DTG; TDF/XTC or TAF/FTC + DRV/r 600/100 mg twice daily). Earlier guidance (EACS 2018) advised against using TAF- and cobicistat-containing regimens as initial therapy in pregnancy due to limited data. EVG/c regimens are not recommended in pregnancy because cobicistat-boosting leads to reduced drug exposure, and RPV/TAF/FTC is not listed among recommended initial options and is less appropriate with high baseline viral load.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 29-year-old woman at 16 weeks’ gestation is newly diagnosed with HIV during routine prenatal screening. She is ART-naïve. HIV-1 RNA is 120,000 copies/mL; CD4 count is 420/µL. She has normal renal and hepatic function and no hepatitis B or C coinfection. She takes only prenatal vitamins. She asks for a once-daily regimen if possible. According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate initial antiretroviral regimen?

Answer.Choice_A: Coformulated tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine/bictegravir once daily
Answer.Choice_B: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine plus raltegravir 400 mg twice daily
Answer.Choice_C: Coformulated elvitegravir/cobicistat/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine once daily
Answer.Choice_D: Tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine plus darunavir/cobicistat once daily
Answer.Explanation: EACS 2025 includes coformulated TAF/FTC/bictegravir once daily among the recommended initial regimens for ART-naïve pregnant women, reflecting new evidence supporting bictegravir use in pregnancy. In earlier guidance, bictegravir was not recommended and raltegravir twice daily with TDF/FTC was a preferred option; however, raltegravir-based regimens are no longer listed among the recommended initial regimens in the 2026 update. Elvitegravir/cobicistat–containing regimens and darunavir/cobicistat are not recommended in pregnancy due to inadequate drug exposures with cobicistat-based boosting. Thus, TAF/FTC/BIC is the recommended choice per EACS 2026.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 32-year-old man is newly diagnosed with HIV-1 infection during a clinic visit for pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) follow-up. He reports taking daily oral tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine but missed multiple doses over the past 2 months; his last HIV test 3 months ago was negative. He has no history of hepatitis B and is hepatitis B surface antigen negative. Baseline laboratory testing shows HIV-1 RNA of 220,000 copies/mL and CD4 count of 360 cells/µL. Renal and hepatic function are normal. Genotypic resistance testing has been sent and results are pending. He is ready to start antiretroviral therapy today.

According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate initial antiretroviral regimen?

Answer.Choice_A: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine plus dolutegravir once daily
Answer.Choice_B: Dolutegravir plus lamivudine once daily
Answer.Choice_C: Defer antiretroviral therapy until genotypic resistance results are available
Answer.Choice_D: Dolutegravir/rilpivirine two-drug regimen once daily
Answer.Explanation: EACS 2025 permits dolutegravir/lamivudine (DTG/3TC) in ART-naïve adults who are HBsAg negative and have HIV-1 RNA <500,000 copies/mL, but specifically advises against DTG/3TC after suspected oral PrEP failure. Because this patient acquired HIV while taking oral TDF/FTC with recent nonadherence (suspected PrEP failure), DTG/3TC should be avoided; a standard triple regimen with two NRTIs plus dolutegravir (e.g., TDF/FTC + DTG) is appropriate. In earlier EACS guidance (2020), DTG/3TC was considered for patients meeting HBsAg negativity, HIV-1 RNA <500,000 copies/mL, and CD4 >200 cells/µL—criteria that this patient meets—highlighting the update that added the PrEP failure restriction and removed the CD4 threshold. Deferring ART is not recommended, and DTG/rilpivirine is not indicated for ART-naïve initiation.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 50-year-old man with well-controlled HIV presents for follow-up blood pressure management. He has been on bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide with an undetectable viral load and a CD4 count of 560/µL. He has no history of diabetes, chronic kidney disease, or atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. He was diagnosed with hypertension 3 months ago after repeated clinic measurements of 146–150/92–94 mmHg and was started on amlodipine 5 mg daily. He reports good adherence and no adverse effects. Home blood pressure averages over the past two weeks are 132/84 mmHg, and today’s clinic blood pressure is 134/86 mmHg. Laboratory testing shows eGFR 92 mL/min/1.73 m² and normal electrolytes; urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio is normal. He asks what blood pressure goal he should aim for moving forward.

According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, which of the following targets is recommended for this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Titrate therapy to achieve a systolic blood pressure of 120–129 mmHg
Answer.Choice_B: Titrate therapy to achieve a blood pressure <130/80 mmHg
Answer.Choice_C: Titrate therapy to achieve a blood pressure <140/90 mmHg
Answer.Choice_D: Titrate therapy to achieve a systolic blood pressure <120 mmHg
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 European AIDS Clinical Society guidance on cardiovascular risk management in people living with HIV adopts a systolic blood pressure target of 120–129 mmHg, aligning with updates from the 2024 ESC/ESH hypertension guideline, while retaining ≥140/90 mmHg as the diagnostic threshold for hypertension. Earlier EACS versions initially recommended <130/80 mmHg for high-risk individuals and expanded that target to all people with HIV by 2019–2020. The newly specified systolic range supersedes the previous <130/80 target and does not call for a diastolic <80 mmHg. A <140/90 mmHg goal is too permissive, and an intensive systolic target <120 mmHg is not recommended by the cited guidance.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 36-year-old man is newly diagnosed with HIV-1 infection. He is ART-naïve and has no significant medical history. Medications include only a daily multivitamin. He denies substance use. Physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show CD4 count 410/µL and HIV-1 RNA 85,000 copies/mL. Serum creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL with an estimated GFR of 105 mL/min/1.73 m²; liver enzymes are normal. Hepatitis B surface antigen is negative, and anti-HBs is positive. HLA-B*57:01 testing and baseline resistance genotyping are pending. He prefers a once-daily regimen with a high barrier to resistance and minimal drug–drug interactions. According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate initial antiretroviral regimen for this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Bictegravir/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine once daily
Answer.Choice_B: Elvitegravir/cobicistat/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine once daily with food
Answer.Choice_C: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine plus efavirenz once daily
Answer.Choice_D: Tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine plus nevirapine once daily
Answer.Explanation: EACS 2025 lists 2 NRTIs + an integrase inhibitor as preferred initial therapy for ART-naïve adults, including bictegravir/TAF/FTC as a recommended once-daily single-tablet regimen with a high resistance barrier and few drug interactions. In contrast, elvitegravir/cobicistat-based combinations (e.g., TAF/FTC/EVG/c) that were previously included as alternative options (EACS 2020) are no longer listed among recommended or alternative initial regimens in the 2026 update. NNRTI-based options such as TDF/FTC + efavirenz or TAF/FTC + nevirapine are not recommended initial regimens due to lower resistance barriers and adverse effect profiles.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 42-year-old man is newly diagnosed with HIV-1 infection during routine screening. He has no prior antiretroviral therapy. He denies cardiovascular disease and does not smoke. Vital signs are normal. Laboratory results show HIV RNA 180,000 copies/mL and CD4 count 430/µL. Serum creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR 110 mL/min/1.73 m²). HBsAg and anti-HBc are negative; anti-HBs is negative. HLA-B*57:01 is negative. A baseline resistance genotype has been sent but results are not yet available. 

According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate initial antiretroviral regimen for this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine plus dolutegravir once daily
Answer.Choice_B: Abacavir/lamivudine plus dolutegravir once daily
Answer.Choice_C: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine plus boosted darunavir once daily
Answer.Choice_D: Abacavir/lamivudine plus raltegravir twice daily
Answer.Explanation: EACS 2025 lists abacavir/lamivudine plus dolutegravir (including the fixed-dose combination) as an alternative—not a recommended—initial regimen, even in patients who are HLA-B*57:01–negative and HBsAg–negative. Recommended first-line options include integrase inhibitor–based triple therapy with tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine plus dolutegravir or bictegravir/TAF/FTC. Therefore, TAF/FTC + dolutegravir is the most appropriate recommended regimen here. In contrast, EACS 2022 had included ABC/3TC + DTG among recommended initial regimens, which explains why selecting that regimen (choice B) reflects an earlier recommendation rather than the 2026 guidance. Choices C and D are medically plausible but are not preferred first-line regimens for a treatment-naive patient without specific indications for a boosted protease inhibitor or for abacavir plus raltegravir.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 29-year-old man who has sex with men has been taking daily oral tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine for HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) but reports missing 2–3 doses per week over the past month. He presents with 5 days of fever, myalgias, and sore throat. A 4th-generation HIV Ag/Ab test is positive, and HIV-1 RNA is 85,000 copies/mL. CD4 count is 510/µL. HBsAg is negative with protective anti-HBs. Creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73 m²). At a follow-up visit 6 days later, baseline genotypic resistance testing shows no NRTI or INSTI resistance (no M184V or K65R; no integrase mutations). He has not yet started antiretroviral therapy. According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate initial antiretroviral regimen in this situation?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate dolutegravir plus lamivudine dual therapy now that baseline genotyping shows no resistance
Answer.Choice_B: Initiate tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine plus boosted darunavir and avoid dolutegravir/lamivudine in this setting
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate doravirine/tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/lamivudine single-tablet triple therapy as initial treatment
Answer.Choice_D: Initiate long-acting cabotegravir/rilpivirine injections as first-line therapy before achieving viral suppression
Answer.Explanation: EACS 2025 (v13.0) allows dual therapy with dolutegravir plus lamivudine/emtricitabine for individuals who acquired HIV while using oral PrEP if baseline genotyping shows no resistance. Earlier guidance (2022) recommended starting a triple-drug regimen with two NRTIs plus a high–genetic barrier agent (e.g., boosted darunavir or a second-generation INSTI) and advised against 3TC/DTG in this context. Because this patient’s genotype shows no NRTI or INSTI resistance and he has no HBV coinfection, DTG+3TC is an acceptable initial option. Doravirine-based triple therapy is not preferred for PrEP acquisition where a high-barrier agent was previously emphasized, and long-acting cabotegravir/rilpivirine is not indicated before achieving viral suppression or without careful resistance assessment.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 29-year-old G1P0 at 12 weeks’ gestation is newly diagnosed with HIV during routine prenatal screening. She is antiretroviral therapy (ART)–naive and has no medical comorbidities. She takes only prenatal vitamins and denies use of over-the-counter or herbal products. Physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory testing shows HIV-1 RNA of 72,000 copies/mL and CD4 count of 420/µL. Serum creatinine is 0.7 mg/dL, liver enzymes are normal, HBsAg and HCV antibody are negative, and interferon-gamma release assay for tuberculosis is negative. HIV genotypic resistance testing shows no clinically significant mutations, including no INSTI resistance. She asks about recommended initial ART options in pregnancy.

According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, which of the following initial antiretroviral regimens is included among the recommended options for ART-naive pregnant women and represents an addition compared with the 2022 version?

Answer.Choice_A: Tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine/bictegravir once daily
Answer.Choice_B: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine plus dolutegravir once daily
Answer.Choice_C: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine/efavirenz once daily
Answer.Choice_D: Tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine plus raltegravir 400 mg once daily
Answer.Explanation: EACS 2025 added TAF/FTC/BIC (bictegravir-based single-tablet regimen) as a recommended initial regimen for ART-naive pregnant women. In the 2022 version, recommended options included TDF/XTC or TAF/FTC + dolutegravir, TDF/XTC or TAF/FTC + darunavir/ritonavir (600/100 mg twice daily), and ABC/3TC + dolutegravir; bictegravir-containing regimens were not listed. Thus, TAF/FTC/BIC once daily is the regimen newly included among recommended options. TDF/FTC + dolutegravir (B) was already recommended previously. TDF/FTC/efavirenz (C) is not a recommended initial regimen in EACS preferred lists for pregnancy. Raltegravir in pregnancy is recommended as 400 mg twice daily; once-daily dosing (D) is not recommended due to reduced exposures in pregnancy.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 30-year-old woman at 12 weeks’ gestation presents for initiation of antiretroviral therapy after a new diagnosis of HIV-1 infection. She is antiretroviral-naive. Baseline HIV-1 RNA is 150,000 copies/mL and CD4 count is 380/µL. Hepatitis B surface antigen is negative, and anti-HBs is positive. Serum creatinine is 0.7 mg/dL (eGFR 110 mL/min/1.73 m²). HLA-B*57:01 testing is negative. She takes prenatal vitamins only and has no other comorbidities or drug interactions. Genotype is pending. She prefers a once-daily regimen if possible.

According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, which of the following initial antiretroviral regimens is recommended for this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine/bictegravir once daily
Answer.Choice_B: Abacavir/lamivudine plus dolutegravir once daily
Answer.Choice_C: Abacavir/lamivudine plus raltegravir twice daily
Answer.Choice_D: Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine plus efavirenz 600 mg once daily
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 European AIDS Clinical Society guideline recommends TAF/FTC/BIC once daily, TDF/XTC or TAF/FTC + dolutegravir, and TDF/XTC or TAF/FTC + darunavir/ritonavir twice daily as initial regimens for ART-naive pregnant women. Abacavir/lamivudine + dolutegravir, which had been listed among recommended options in 2022, is no longer included in the recommended list. Abacavir/lamivudine + raltegravir and TDF/FTC + efavirenz are medically plausible but are not among the recommended initial regimens in the 2026 guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
An 18-month-old boy with newly diagnosed HIV-1 infection presents to initiate antiretroviral therapy. He weighs 10 kg, has normal renal and hepatic function, is HBsAg negative and up to date on immunizations, and has no tuberculosis exposure or concomitant medications. Baseline genotype shows no major resistance mutations, including no integrase resistance. The team plans to use a recommended NRTI backbone and a guideline-preferred anchor drug for his age group.

According to the European AIDS Clinical Society guideline issued in 2025, in addition to dolutegravir, which anchor drug is recommended as a preferred option for children aged 4 weeks–3 years?

Answer.Choice_A: Bictegravir
Answer.Choice_B: Dolutegravir
Answer.Choice_C: Darunavir/ritonavir
Answer.Choice_D: Raltegravir
Answer.Explanation: EACS 2025 lists bictegravir and dolutegravir as preferred anchor drugs for children aged 4 weeks–3 years, representing an addition of bictegravir to the preferred options. Earlier versions listed dolutegravir as the sole preferred agent in this age group. Darunavir/ritonavir and raltegravir are plausible alternatives in pediatrics but are not listed as preferred anchors for this specific age bracket in the cited guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 62-year-old woman with hypertension and hyperlipidemia is referred for vasodilator stress cardiac MRI to evaluate exertional chest pressure. The lab plans quantitative first-pass myocardial perfusion mapping. On a similar study last month using a single-bolus sequence, the left ventricular blood-pool signal clipped at peak contrast, compromising arterial input function (AIF) quantification. The scanner has recently been upgraded with a vendor-implemented dual-sequence option that provides a dedicated AIF image with gradient-echo readout, linear k-space ordering, and adjustable saturation-recovery time. Renal function is normal. 

According to the Society for Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate AIF acquisition strategy to minimize blood-pool saturation for quantitative analysis?

Answer.Choice_A: Implement a dual-sequence perfusion protocol with a dedicated AIF image using GRE readout with linear k-space ordering and a saturation-recovery time <30 ms
Answer.Choice_B: Implement a dual-bolus protocol deriving the AIF from a low-dose pre-bolus and adjust contrast dose and delivery to minimize saturation
Answer.Choice_C: Acquire a single-bolus dataset and estimate the AIF from the LV cavity using post-processing corrections for signal nonlinearity without changing the sequence
Answer.Choice_D: Place the AIF region of interest in the right ventricular cavity and use a spin-echo readout with a longer saturation-recovery time (>50 ms) to maximize blood-pool signal
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SCMR guideline recommends using a dual-sequence technique for AIF acquisition—with a dedicated low-saturation AIF image using GRE readout, linear ordering, and maximum TS <30 ms—and prefers this approach whenever available because it is more robust and easier in routine practice. A dual-bolus method is a possible alternative only if a dual-sequence implementation is unavailable; earlier guidance emphasized dual-bolus to avoid blood-pool saturation. Single-bolus with post-processing correction and RV-based AIF with spin-echo/long TS are not recommended.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 64-year-old woman with hypertension and hyperlipidemia undergoes vasodilator stress cardiovascular magnetic resonance for evaluation of exertional chest discomfort. Quantitative first-pass perfusion is planned with three short-axis myocardial slices and an LV cavity arterial input function (AIF) using a dual-sequence approach. At peak regadenoson stress, her heart rate is 118/min. With the available sequence and target readout window, the team cannot acquire both the AIF and all three myocardial slices within each RR interval without exceeding the temporal acquisition window. The technologist asks whether to switch to alternate-heartbeat imaging to accommodate additional slices. According to the Society for Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance guideline issued in 2025, which acquisition strategy should be used to enable valid quantitative perfusion in this circumstance?

Answer.Choice_A: Prioritize acquisition of the AIF every heartbeat and acquire myocardial slices on alternate heartbeats
Answer.Choice_B: Use a temporal resolution of 1–2 RR intervals for both AIF and myocardium, as this is acceptable for quantitative perfusion
Answer.Choice_C: Acquire myocardial images every heartbeat while sampling the AIF on alternate heartbeats
Answer.Choice_D: Acquire both the AIF and myocardial images on alternate heartbeats to accommodate more slices
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SCMR guideline specifies that quantitative perfusion should use a temporal resolution equivalent to 1 RR interval, with at least the arterial input function sampled every heartbeat and ideally the myocardium as well. Acquisition at alternate heartbeats to add slices is not recommended for quantitative analysis. However, if stress tachycardia prevents obtaining both the AIF and all myocardial slices every beat, the AIF should be prioritized every heartbeat, with myocardial slices acquired on alternate beats. Options B, C, and D do not align with these recommendations: accepting 1–2 RR intervals, sampling the AIF every other beat, or alternating both signals would compromise quantitative accuracy.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 64-year-old woman with hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hyperlipidemia reports exertional chest pressure when walking uphill. Resting ECG shows left bundle branch block. She undergoes vasodilator stress perfusion cardiovascular magnetic resonance (CMR). Imaging demonstrates a reversible perfusion defect involving the mid-to-apical anterior and anteroseptal walls without late gadolinium enhancement; left ventricular ejection fraction is 58%. The interpreting cardiologist is finalizing the report and wants to quantify the extent of inducible ischemia in a way aligned with contemporary standards.

According to the Society for Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is recommended for reporting the extent of the inducible perfusion defect?

Answer.Choice_A: Report the ischemic burden as the percent of total left ventricular myocardium demonstrating inducible perfusion abnormality
Answer.Choice_B: Report the ischemic burden by listing the number of AHA segments with inducible perfusion defects and describing transmurality and distribution
Answer.Choice_C: Report the ischemic burden by documenting the number of coronary territories with inducible perfusion defects without estimating size
Answer.Choice_D: Report the ischemic burden using a summed visual stress perfusion score across myocardial segments
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 Society for Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance guidance recommends quantifying the extent of inducible perfusion defects as the percent of myocardium affected, facilitating standardized ischemic burden reporting. Earlier guidance (2013) emphasized counting involved AHA segments with comments on transmurality and distribution. Options C and D are plausible approaches but are not the recommended metric for quantifying extent in the current guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 64-year-old man with hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hyperlipidemia has exertional chest discomfort limiting daily activities. Coronary CT angiography shows multivessel calcification with borderline (40%–60%) stenoses in the left anterior descending and right coronary arteries. He undergoes vasodilator stress myocardial perfusion CMR with dual-sequence acquisition and automated motion correction to quantify myocardial blood flow and myocardial perfusion reserve, as balanced ischemia and subtle subendocardial defects are clinical concerns. The imaging lab is deciding on the analysis workflow for perfusion quantification.

According to the Society for Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance consensus statement issued in 2025, which of the following analysis strategies should be used to quantify perfusion in this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Perform pixel-wise (voxel-wise) perfusion quantification to generate high-resolution myocardial blood flow maps
Answer.Choice_B: Perform segment-wise quantification by averaging signal-intensity curves within standardized AHA myocardial segments
Answer.Choice_C: Compute a single global left ventricular myocardial blood flow value by averaging the entire myocardium
Answer.Choice_D: Use semi-quantitative upslope analysis limited to the midventricular short-axis slice without absolute quantification
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SCMR consensus recommends pixel-wise perfusion quantification to preserve spatial resolution and improve detection of subtle subendocardial ischemia; segment-wise averaging is explicitly not recommended. Earlier workflows emphasized segment-based analysis of AHA segments, which sacrifices spatial resolution. Global averaging or limited semi-quantitative mid-slice analysis are plausible but do not align with the consensus recommendation and may miss regional subendocardial abnormalities.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 64-year-old man with exertional chest pain is referred for vasodilator stress cardiovascular magnetic resonance at 1.5 T with fully quantitative myocardial perfusion analysis (ml/min/g). The laboratory has both dual-sequence and dual-bolus capabilities; the scanner supports simultaneous low–flip-angle imaging of the left ventricular blood pool while acquiring standard saturation-recovery myocardial perfusion images. The patient is in sinus rhythm and can comply with breath-holding. According to the Society for Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance guideline issued in 2025, which of the following arterial input function acquisition strategies should be used?

Answer.Choice_A: Acquire the arterial input function with a dual-sequence technique; if dual-sequence is unavailable, use a dual-bolus approach
Answer.Choice_B: Acquire the arterial input function with either a dual-bolus or a dual-sequence technique, with no preference between them
Answer.Choice_C: Acquire the arterial input function with a single-bolus, single-sequence approach and apply post hoc signal–concentration linearization
Answer.Choice_D: Acquire the arterial input function using a high-dose single bolus and estimate it from the left ventricular cavity on standard saturation-recovery images
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 Society for Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance guidance explicitly prefers a dual-sequence technique for arterial input function acquisition for quantitative perfusion because it is more robust and easier to implement in clinical practice; a dual-bolus approach is acceptable only when dual-sequence is not available. The 2020 guidance treated dual-bolus and dual-sequence as equivalent options. Single-bolus, single-sequence methods with post hoc linearization or high-dose single-bolus AIF estimation are not recommended approaches in these guidelines.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 62-year-old man is referred for adenosine stress cardiac MRI to evaluate exertional chest discomfort. Quantitative first-pass perfusion mapping is planned. He is cooperative and can perform reliable 12–15 second breath-holds. The scanner has sequences that enable free-shallow-breathing acquisitions with integrated non-rigid motion correction as part of the reconstruction pipeline, applicable to both arterial input function (AIF) and myocardial images. The technologist asks whether to instruct the patient to breath-hold or to allow free breathing. According to the Society for Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance guideline issued in 2025, which of the following respiratory and motion strategies should be used?

Answer.Choice_A: Acquire stress and rest first-pass perfusion during free shallow breathing using sequences with integrated non-rigid motion correction for both AIF and myocardial images
Answer.Choice_B: Instruct the patient to breath-hold starting just before contrast reaches the left ventricular cavity for both stress and rest passes, using motion correction only if breath-hold breaks occur
Answer.Choice_C: Acquire free-breathing datasets without motion correction and perform rigid registration during post-processing only for myocardial images; obtain the AIF during a breath-hold
Answer.Choice_D: Use respiratory navigator-gated acquisitions during quiet breathing without motion correction, accepting longer scan times to minimize diaphragmatic motion
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 Society for Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance guideline recommends free-shallow-breathing first-pass perfusion with integrated non-rigid motion correction whenever available, and specifies that motion correction should be applied to both the AIF and myocardial images to support robust quantitative perfusion. Breath-hold acquisitions are reserved as an alternative when motion correction is unavailable. The earlier approach emphasizing breath-holding beginning before contrast reached the LV cavity has been superseded by free-breathing with integrated motion correction for quantitative workflows.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 49-year-old woman presents with de novo metastatic triple-negative breast cancer involving the liver and lungs. Biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma that is ER- and PR-negative and HER2 0 by IHC. PD-L1 testing shows immune cell staining of 2% by SP142 and a combined positive score (CPS) of 12 by the 22C3 assay. She has an ECOG performance status of 0, no history of autoimmune disease, and adequate organ function. She has not received systemic therapy for metastatic disease.

According to the Spanish Society of Medical Oncology (SEOM), Spanish Breast Cancer Research Group (GEICAM), and SOLTI guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate pembrolizumab plus gemcitabine and carboplatin
Answer.Choice_B: Initiate atezolizumab plus nab-paclitaxel as a preferred first-line regimen
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate single-agent chemotherapy without immunotherapy
Answer.Choice_D: Initiate atezolizumab plus paclitaxel
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SEOM/GEICAM/SOLTI guideline states that pembrolizumab combined with chemotherapy is considered the standard-of-care first-line treatment for PD-L1–positive metastatic TNBC, based on improved PFS and OS in phase 3 trials (e.g., KEYNOTE-355 for CPS ≥10). Atezolizumab combinations, previously considered a preferred option when PD-L1 was positive by SP142, now have limited use due to conflicting results from IMpassion130 and IMpassion131; therefore, atezolizumab plus nab-paclitaxel (prior preferred option) is no longer recommended as first-line standard therapy. Single-agent chemotherapy is not preferred in PD-L1–positive disease, and atezolizumab plus paclitaxel is not recommended given the negative IMpassion131 trial.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 58-year-old woman with ER-positive, PR-positive, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer presents with worsening right upper quadrant discomfort and dyspnea on exertion. Four years ago, she underwent lumpectomy and radiation for stage II disease and received adjuvant aromatase inhibitor therapy. She developed metastatic recurrence 18 months ago and was treated with palbociclib plus fulvestrant for 14 months until radiographic and clinical progression. Current imaging shows multiple liver and bilateral pulmonary metastases. ECOG performance status is 1. Laboratory studies show total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, AST 78 U/L, ALT 64 U/L, and alkaline phosphatase 310 U/L. The oncology team plans to start the first chemotherapy line for advanced disease due to symptomatic, endocrine-refractory progression. The patient asks whether adding bevacizumab to chemotherapy would improve outcomes.

According to the SEOM–GEICAM–SOLTI clinical practice guideline issued in 2025 for HR-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer, which of the following is the most appropriate initial systemic therapy approach?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate weekly paclitaxel monotherapy without bevacizumab
Answer.Choice_B: Initiate bevacizumab plus weekly paclitaxel as first-line chemotherapy
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate ribociclib plus letrozole as first-line systemic therapy
Answer.Choice_D: Initiate doxorubicin plus cyclophosphamide combination chemotherapy
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 joint SEOM–GEICAM–SOLTI guideline for HR-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer removed the previous consideration of bevacizumab plus chemotherapy as a first-line option; there is no longer a recommendation to add bevacizumab in this setting. Thus, initiating single-agent chemotherapy without bevacizumab (e.g., weekly paclitaxel) is guideline-concordant. The 2023 guidance allowed bevacizumab plus chemotherapy to be considered in selected patients because of PFS benefit without OS benefit, but this has been explicitly removed in the 2026 update. Endocrine-based therapy with a CDK4/6 inhibitor plus an aromatase inhibitor (Choice C) is not appropriate here given recent progression on endocrine therapy with a CDK4/6 inhibitor and the need for chemotherapy. Combination anthracycline-based chemotherapy (Choice D) is a plausible regimen but is not specifically favored over sequential single-agent chemotherapy in this clinical context, and the key guideline change pertains to avoiding bevacizumab with first-line chemotherapy.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 63-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with progressive low back pain. Imaging shows multiple osteoblastic lesions in the thoracic and lumbar spine without visceral crisis. CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis shows no measurable visceral disease. Core biopsy of a vertebral lesion confirms metastatic invasive ductal carcinoma that is estrogen receptor–positive (90%), progesterone receptor–positive (40%), and HER2-negative (IHC 0). She has no significant comorbidities and has not received endocrine therapy or chemotherapy for metastatic disease. She previously had a stage I, ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer resected 9 years ago and did not receive adjuvant endocrine therapy. Laboratory studies are within normal limits. 

According to the SEOM–GEICAM–SOLTI clinical practice guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate first-line systemic therapy?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate an aromatase inhibitor plus a CDK4/6 inhibitor (eg, letrozole with ribociclib)
Answer.Choice_B: Initiate fulvestrant plus a CDK4/6 inhibitor (eg, fulvestrant with ribociclib)
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate aromatase inhibitor monotherapy (eg, letrozole alone)
Answer.Choice_D: Initiate single-agent chemotherapy (eg, weekly paclitaxel)
Answer.Explanation: For postmenopausal patients with HR-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer without visceral crisis and no prior endocrine therapy in the metastatic setting, first-line treatment should combine an aromatase inhibitor with a CDK4/6 inhibitor. The SEOM–GEICAM–SOLTI 2025 guideline update removes the prior explicit endorsement of fulvestrant-based CDK4/6 inhibitor combinations as first-line options, narrowing first-line recommendations primarily to AI-based combinations with CDK4/6 inhibitors. While earlier guidance (2023) allowed fulvestrant plus ribociclib or abemaciclib as adequate first-line options based on MONALEESA-3 and MONARCH-2, and PARSIFAL showed no advantage of fulvestrant–palbociclib over letrozole–palbociclib, the 2026 update no longer lists fulvestrant-based combinations as recommended first-line regimens. Therefore, AI plus CDK4/6 inhibitor is preferred; AI monotherapy or chemotherapy are less appropriate without visceral crisis.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 63-year-old woman is newly diagnosed with de novo metastatic breast cancer involving the liver and bone. Core biopsy of a breast mass shows invasive ductal carcinoma that is ER-positive (90%), PR-positive (40%), and HER2-negative (IHC 0; FISH nonamplified). She has an ECOG performance status of 1 and no visceral crisis. The oncology team plans to start letrozole plus a CDK4/6 inhibitor. She asks whether broader genomic testing beyond standard receptor assays should be done now to help guide therapy. 

According to the 2025 SEOM–GEICAM–SOLTI metastatic breast cancer guideline, which of the following is recommended?

Answer.Choice_A: Obtain tumor multigene NGS and/or ctDNA profiling now to assist in identifying actionable alterations (eg, PIK3CA, ESR1, BRCA1/2, HER2 mutations; MSI-high or high TMB; NTRK fusions)
Answer.Choice_B: Do not order multigene NGS or ctDNA profiling because these tests are not routinely recommended; reserve them only if results would alter immediate treatment or enable clinical trial enrollment
Answer.Choice_C: Limit testing to germline BRCA1/2 only and avoid tumor multigene NGS or ctDNA profiling in the metastatic setting because they lack proven clinical utility
Answer.Choice_D: Defer genomic testing until radiographic progression and then consider targeted single-gene assays only if a clinical trial is being considered
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SEOM–GEICAM–SOLTI guideline states that next-generation sequencing and/or ctDNA genomic profiling could be considered to assist in identifying actionable alterations in metastatic breast cancer, including PIK3CA, ESR1, BRCA1/2, and HER2 mutations, MSI-high status, high tumor mutational burden, and NTRK fusions. This reflects a shift from earlier guidance that did not recommend routine use except when results would immediately change treatment or enable clinical trial enrollment. Options B, C, and D are more restrictive and do not align with the updated recommendation to consider NGS/ctDNA profiling for actionable targets.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 49-year-old woman is newly diagnosed with de novo metastatic, ER-negative/HER2-positive breast cancer with biopsy-proven liver and lung metastases. She denies headache, visual changes, dizziness, gait imbalance, nausea, or seizures. Neurologic examination is normal. She is scheduled to start first-line HER2-directed therapy and asks whether she should have a brain scan now to look for metastases, given her tumor subtype.

According to the SEOM–GEICAM–SOLTI guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate approach to screening for central nervous system (CNS) metastases in this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Defer CNS imaging; do not perform screening neuroimaging in the absence of neurologic symptoms or signs
Answer.Choice_B: Obtain a contrast-enhanced brain MRI now to screen for occult CNS metastases because HER2-positive disease confers higher risk and results could alter therapy
Answer.Choice_C: Obtain a noncontrast head CT now as a pragmatic screening test for CNS metastases
Answer.Choice_D: Schedule surveillance brain MRI every 6–12 months to monitor for asymptomatic CNS progression given HER2-positive status
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SEOM–GEICAM–SOLTI advanced breast cancer guideline states that central nervous system screening in asymptomatic patients is not routinely recommended. This applies across breast cancer subtypes, including HER2-positive disease. The previous (2023) guidance allowed consideration of screening in higher-risk subtypes (HER2-positive or triple-negative) if findings would alter treatment, but this caveat has been removed. Brain imaging is reserved for patients with neurologic symptoms or signs. Noncontrast head CT is suboptimal for detecting small metastases, and routine surveillance MRI in asymptomatic patients is not recommended due to lack of demonstrated benefit and potential harms (false positives, cost, anxiety).
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 48-year-old woman with hormone receptor–positive, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer on systemic therapy presents with 2 weeks of progressive left hand clumsiness and headaches. Neurologic examination shows mild left pronator drift. Brain MRI reveals a single 3.2-cm enhancing right frontal lobe lesion with surrounding vasogenic edema and no other intracranial metastases. There is no leptomeningeal enhancement. Systemic disease is otherwise stable. The lesion is deemed surgically resectable to relieve mass effect and obtain tissue for molecular profiling. According to the SEOM–GEICAM–SOLTI clinical practice guideline issued in 2025 for management of brain metastases, which of the following local treatment strategies is most appropriate?

Answer.Choice_A: Preoperative stereotactic radiosurgery to the intact lesion followed by planned surgical resection
Answer.Choice_B: Gross total surgical resection followed by postoperative cavity stereotactic radiosurgery
Answer.Choice_C: Gross total surgical resection followed by whole-brain radiotherapy
Answer.Choice_D: Gross total surgical resection alone without adjuvant radiation
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SEOM–GEICAM–SOLTI guideline states that SRS is the primary treatment for single or a limited number (≤10) of brain metastases and that, for patients undergoing resection, postoperative SRS is an alternative to whole-brain radiotherapy. However, preoperative SRS may be favored due to lower risks of radiation necrosis and leptomeningeal disease. Earlier guidance typically emphasized postoperative cavity SRS or WBRT rather than preoperative SRS. Surgery alone has higher local failure rates, and WBRT carries a greater risk of neurocognitive decline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 54-year-old woman with de novo metastatic, ER/PR-negative, HER2-positive breast cancer (IHC 3+) achieved a partial response for 11 months on first-line docetaxel, trastuzumab, and pertuzumab. She now presents with right upper quadrant discomfort and fatigue. Examination shows mild hepatomegaly without jaundice. CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis reveals progression with multiple new hepatic lesions and increased size of known lesions; there is no evidence of pulmonary infiltrates or brain metastases. ECOG performance status is 1. Left ventricular ejection fraction is 62%. She has no history of interstitial lung disease. She has not received antibody–drug conjugates or small-molecule HER2 TKIs. 

According to the 2025 joint guideline from the Spanish Society of Medical Oncology (SEOM), the Spanish Breast Cancer Research Group (GEICAM), and SOLTI, which of the following is the most appropriate second-line therapy?

Answer.Choice_A: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) monotherapy
Answer.Choice_B: Trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1) monotherapy
Answer.Choice_C: Lapatinib plus capecitabine
Answer.Choice_D: Tucatinib plus trastuzumab plus capecitabine
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SEOM–GEICAM–SOLTI guideline, based on DESTINY-Breast03, designates trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) as the preferred second-line therapy for patients with metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer who progress on a trastuzumab-containing regimen, given superior progression-free and overall survival compared with T-DM1. Earlier guidance recommended T-DM1 as second line based on EMILIA; however, T-DXd has supplanted T-DM1 in this setting. Lapatinib–capecitabine is inferior and not preferred as second line. Tucatinib with trastuzumab and capecitabine is generally used in later lines (often after prior antibody–drug conjugate exposure) and is not the recommended second-line option in this scenario.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 64-year-old man with stage III colon adenocarcinoma on adjuvant capecitabine presents to clinic 36 hours after onset of sore throat, dry cough, and fever. A SARS-CoV-2 antigen test is positive. He is fully ambulatory and not hypoxic (SpO2 98% on room air). Temperature is 38.1°C, blood pressure is 132/78 mm Hg, and heart rate is 92/min. He has no dyspnea or chest pain. Medications include capecitabine and amlodipine. Serum creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR 85 mL/min/1.73 m2); hepatic panel is normal. He has no known drug allergies. 

According to the Infectious Diseases Working Party (AGIHO) of the German Society for Hematology and Medical Oncology guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this non-hospitalized cancer patient with mild COVID-19?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate nirmatrelvir/ritonavir orally for 5 days as soon as possible, ideally within 72 hours of symptom onset
Answer.Choice_B: Defer antiviral therapy and provide symptomatic care only, avoiding outpatient use of remdesivir or other agents
Answer.Choice_C: Start dexamethasone 6 mg orally daily for 10 days without antiviral therapy
Answer.Choice_D: Begin hydroxychloroquine plus azithromycin in the outpatient setting
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 AGIHO/DGHO guideline recommends early outpatient antiviral therapy for non-hospitalized cancer patients with mild COVID-19, with nirmatrelvir/ritonavir as first-line if started ideally within 72 hours of symptom onset; if not feasible, a 3-day remdesivir course is recommended. Earlier guidance (2020) advised against outpatient antivirals in ambulatory cancer patients. Dexamethasone and hydroxychloroquine (with or without azithromycin) are not recommended for mild outpatient disease.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 69-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer on endocrine therapy is hospitalized on day 8 of symptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection for worsening hypoxemia. She requires 6 L/min nasal cannula to maintain an SpO2 of 92%. Temperature is 38.2°C. Chest radiograph shows bilateral patchy opacities. Laboratory studies show CRP 165 mg/L, ferritin 1400 ng/mL, D-dimer 1.2 µg/mL, WBC 6,200/µL (neutrophils 70%), creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR 80 mL/min/1.73 m2), and normal transaminases. Blood cultures are pending and she has no signs of bacterial sepsis. Remdesivir has been started. 

According to the Infectious Diseases Working Party (AGIHO) of the German Society for Hematology and Medical Oncology (DGHO) guideline issued in 2025, which of the following interventions should be initiated for immunomodulation in this oxygen-requiring, systemically inflamed patient with cancer?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate dexamethasone plus baricitinib
Answer.Choice_B: Initiate dexamethasone alone and avoid baricitinib in oxygen-requiring disease
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate dexamethasone plus tocilizumab
Answer.Choice_D: Initiate dexamethasone plus anakinra
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 AGIHO/DGHO guideline elevates baricitinib to first-line immunomodulatory therapy, in addition to dexamethasone, for hospitalized cancer patients with severe COVID-19 requiring oxygen and demonstrating systemic inflammation. Tocilizumab or anakinra are alternatives when JAK inhibition is unavailable or contraindicated. In contrast, the 2020 guidance strongly recommended dexamethasone for oxygen-requiring patients and did not recommend baricitinib for such patients (limited to non–oxygen-requiring), with only marginal support for tocilizumab in hyperinflammation.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 64-year-old man with chronic lymphocytic leukemia receiving a Bruton tyrosine kinase inhibitor and who completed five doses of mRNA COVID-19 vaccine presents for preventive care. Despite vaccination, his anti–SARS-CoV-2 spike IgG remains undetectable. He has not had COVID-19. Regional sequencing reports indicate that JN.1 sublineage variants with the F456L spike mutation predominate locally. He asks about receiving a preventive monoclonal antibody before the upcoming winter season and specifically mentions pemivibart, which he read about online. According to the Infectious Diseases Working Party (AGIHO) of the German Society for Hematology and Medical Oncology guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate management?

Answer.Choice_A: Do not administer any pre-exposure anti–SARS-CoV-2 monoclonal antibody (e.g., pemivibart or sipavibart) because F456L-predominant variants are resistant
Answer.Choice_B: Administer a long-acting anti–SARS-CoV-2 monoclonal antibody that retains activity against the locally predominant variant for pre-exposure prophylaxis
Answer.Choice_C: Administer tixagevimab/cilgavimab for pre-exposure prophylaxis using the PROVENT regimen
Answer.Choice_D: Administer pemivibart for pre-exposure prophylaxis now and repeat dosing every 6 months regardless of local variant patterns
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 AGIHO/DGHO guideline advises against pre-exposure prophylaxis with anti–SARS-CoV-2 monoclonal antibodies in cancer patients with inadequate vaccine response because predominant JN.1 sublineage variants bearing F456L confer resistance. Earlier products (e.g., tixagevimab/cilgavimab) and newer agents (pemivibart, sipavibart) no longer retain adequate neutralizing activity and are not recommended. In 2023, pre-exposure prophylaxis with a long-acting monoclonal antibody active against circulating variants was suggested for poorly responding patients, but this has been withdrawn due to variant escape.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 67-year-old man with metastatic non–small cell lung cancer receiving maintenance pembrolizumab presents with 7 days of fever, cough, and dyspnea. He tests positive for SARS-CoV-2 and is admitted with hypoxemia requiring 4 L/min nasal cannula (SpO2 91% on room air). He is alert, not intubated, and has bilateral patchy opacities on chest radiograph. Laboratory studies show CRP 168 mg/L, ferritin 1200 ng/mL, and D-dimer 1.2 µg/mL. Serum creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR 85 mL/min/1.73 m2), and AST/ALT are mildly elevated at 55/60 U/L. He has received 2 doses of dexamethasone 6 mg daily. There is no history of chronic liver disease, active bacterial infection, or thrombosis.

According to the Infectious Diseases Working Party (AGIHO) of the German Society for Hematology and Medical Oncology (DGHO) guideline issued in 2025, which of the following immunomodulatory strategies should be initiated now?

Answer.Choice_A: Add baricitinib to ongoing dexamethasone
Answer.Choice_B: Add tocilizumab to ongoing dexamethasone
Answer.Choice_C: Add tofacitinib to ongoing dexamethasone
Answer.Choice_D: Switch to high-dose methylprednisolone without additional agents
Answer.Explanation: He has severe COVID-19 (oxygen requirement, WHO 5–6) with systemic inflammation and no contraindication to JAK inhibition. The 2025 AGIHO/DGHO guideline recommends baricitinib as first-line immunomodulatory therapy in addition to dexamethasone in such patients, with tocilizumab or anakinra as alternatives if JAK inhibition is unavailable or contraindicated. A earlier approach prioritized tocilizumab with dexamethasone and considered JAK inhibitors only as alternatives. Tofacitinib has less robust evidence than baricitinib and is not preferred. Escalating to high-dose methylprednisolone is not recommended over dexamethasone in this setting.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 66-year-old man with metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma receiving FOLFIRINOX is admitted with severe COVID-19 pneumonia requiring mechanical ventilation. He has no history of recent bleeding or surgery. On ICU day 2, his vital signs are stable on ventilatory support. Laboratory studies show platelets 182,000/µL, hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, INR 1.0, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and D-dimer 2.9 µg/mL FEU. Lower-extremity duplex ultrasound shows no deep venous thrombosis. He is hemodynamically stable with low bleeding risk. According to the Infectious Diseases Working Party (AGIHO) of the German Society for Hematology and Medical Oncology (DGHO) guideline issued in 2025, which of the following anticoagulation strategies should be initiated to prevent thromboembolic complications and reduce mortality?

Answer.Choice_A: Administer intermediate-dose low–molecular-weight heparin given severe illness and low bleeding risk
Answer.Choice_B: Administer standard prophylactic-dose low–molecular-weight heparin and avoid escalation to intermediate or therapeutic dosing
Answer.Choice_C: Administer therapeutic-dose low–molecular-weight heparin because of ICU status and elevated D-dimer
Answer.Choice_D: Withhold pharmacologic anticoagulation and use intermittent pneumatic compression only
Answer.Explanation: Per the 2025 AGIHO/DGHO guideline for patients with cancer hospitalized with severe COVID-19 (high-flow oxygen/NIV, mechanical ventilation, or ECMO), intermediate-dose anticoagulation should be considered when bleeding risk is low (BIIt). Routine therapeutic-intensity anticoagulation remains discouraged (DIIt). Earlier guidance discouraged intermediate dosing, favoring prophylactic dosing; however, the update supports intermediate intensity in this severe cohort with low bleeding risk. Mechanical prophylaxis alone is reserved for patients with high bleeding risk, not this patient.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 62-year-old man with relapsed diffuse large B-cell lymphoma is scheduled to receive CD19-directed CAR T-cell therapy in 3 days. He completed bridging chemotherapy and is due to start lymphodepleting cyclophosphamide/fludarabine tomorrow. He reports no fever, cough, rhinorrhea, dyspnea, sore throat, or myalgias, and he has had no recent sick contacts. Vital signs are normal, and lung examination is clear. According to the American Society of Transplantation and Cellular Therapy guideline issued in 2024, which of the following is the most appropriate approach to pre–CAR T respiratory viral testing in this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Defer routine respiratory viral PCR (including SARS-CoV-2) and proceed; test only if respiratory symptoms develop
Answer.Choice_B: Obtain a nasopharyngeal SARS-CoV-2 PCR within 72 hours of lymphodepletion regardless of symptoms
Answer.Choice_C: Perform a point-of-care rapid SARS-CoV-2 antigen test immediately prior to lymphodepletion and omit PCR if negative
Answer.Choice_D: Obtain a multiplex respiratory viral PCR panel (including SARS-CoV-2) within 72 hours in all candidates regardless of symptoms
Answer.Explanation: Per ASTCT 2024 guidance, patients with respiratory symptoms should undergo multiplex respiratory viral PCR (including SARS-CoV-2) and imaging as indicated; the utility of screening asymptomatic adult CAR T candidates is unclear, and many centers test only symptomatic patients. Therefore, an asymptomatic adult need not undergo routine pre–lymphodepletion SARS-CoV-2 or respiratory viral PCR. The 2022 ASTCT guidance encouraged universal screening within 72 hours of conditioning regardless of symptoms (choice B). Choices C and D propose alternative universal or antigen-only screening strategies that are not recommended by the 2024 guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 62-year-old man with mid-rectal adenocarcinoma initially staged as cT3N1 (6 cm from the anal verge) completed total neoadjuvant therapy with long-course chemoradiation followed by consolidation FOLFOX. Twelve weeks after completing therapy, restaging shows: digital rectal exam without a palpable mass, flexible sigmoidoscopy demonstrating a flat whitening scar without ulceration or nodularity with negative bite biopsies, pelvic MRI with no residual tumor (mrTRG1), no suspicious lymph nodes, and normalization of CEA. He has good performance status and strongly prefers to avoid a permanent stoma if oncologically safe. Multidisciplinary review confirms a well-documented clinical complete response. According to the SEOM/GEMCAD/TTD guideline issued in 2025, which of the following is the most appropriate management to present to this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Offer non-operative management with a structured watch-and-wait surveillance program as a therapeutic option
Answer.Choice_B: Recommend immediate total mesorectal excision and restrict non-operative management to prospective trials or highly selected cases after multidisciplinary review
Answer.Choice_C: Perform transanal full-thickness local excision of the scar to obtain pathologic confirmation prior to further decision-making
Answer.Choice_D: Administer additional consolidation systemic therapy for several cycles to maximize pathological response before definitive treatment
Answer.Explanation: The 2025 SEOM/GEMCAD/TTD guideline states that for individuals with a well-documented clinical complete response after neoadjuvant therapy, watch-and-wait yields oncologic outcomes comparable to surgery and should be presented as a therapeutic option. Earlier guidance (2022) did not formally recommend non-operative management and suggested restricting it to clinical trials or exceptional, highly selected cases after multidisciplinary evaluation. Transanal local excision of a clinically complete response scar and extending systemic therapy are not recommended strategies in this scenario.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 39-year-old otorhinolaryngologist applies for the Head and Neck Surgical Oncology expert certificate. Over the past 24 months, she has worked full-time at a regional hospital that is not certified by the German Cancer Society (DKG) as a head and neck tumor center. Her department provides multidisciplinary head and neck cancer care with a weekly interdisciplinary case conference and formal collaboration with radiation oncology, medical oncology, pathology, radiology, and maxillofacial surgery. The hospital has written, defined treatment pathways for common head and neck malignancies. Documentation from the hospital confirms these structures.

According to the German Society of Otorhinolaryngology, Head and Neck Surgery (DGHNO-KHC) and German ORL Academy (DAHNO) qualification criteria issued in 2026, which of the following is the most appropriate determination regarding whether her institutional experience fulfills the required setting criterion for the certificate?

Answer.Choice_A: Count the 24 months as fulfilling the institutional criterion because the hospital qualifies as a comparable institution with regular interdisciplinary case conferences and defined collaborative treatment pathways with main cooperation partners.
Answer.Choice_B: Do not count the 24 months unless they were completed in a DKG-certified head and neck tumor center; comparable institutions are not acceptable for this criterion.
Answer.Choice_C: Count the 24 months only if she also documents a minimum annual head and neck cancer case volume threshold; structural conferences and pathways are insufficient without a volume benchmark.
Answer.Choice_D: Require an additional 12 months in a university-based DKG-certified center, as non-certified institutions cannot fulfill the criterion regardless of their interdisciplinary structures.
Answer.Explanation: The 2026 DGHNO-KHC/DAHNO update broadens eligibility for the institutional activity criterion: in addition to DKG-certified head and neck tumor centers, experience in a comparable institution qualifies if defined structural features are present (regular interdisciplinary case conferences and collaboration with main cooperation partners with defined treatment pathways). The earlier version limited eligibility to activity in a DKG-certified center. Options C and D introduce requirements (case volume thresholds, university affiliation/extra duration) that are not specified by the guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 42-year-old board-certified otolaryngologist in Germany is completing his application for the Expert Certificate in Head and Neck Surgical Oncology. Over the past two years, he helped organize a national oncology-focused continuing education course through the German ORL Academy (DAHNO), delivered two talks at the DGHNO-KHC Oncology Working Group, and served as a subinvestigator in a multicenter study coordinated by the German ENT Study Center (DSZ-HNO). He asks what documentation is needed to fulfill the training/study participation component of the certificate application.

According to the German Society of Otorhinolaryngology, Head and Neck Surgery (DGHNO-KHC) and the German ORL Academy (DAHNO) guidance issued in 2026, which of the following is recommended?

Answer.Choice_A: Provide evidence of participation in or organization of oncology-focused continuing education events or studies, for example through the DGHNO-KHC Oncology Working Group, DAHNO, or the German ENT Study Center (DSZ-HNO)
Answer.Choice_B: Provide evidence of at least five oncology-focused continuing education events and at least three participations in clinical studies and/or scientific activities in head and neck oncology
Answer.Choice_C: Provide evidence of at least ten certified CME credit hours in head and neck surgical oncology from any provider, with no study involvement necessary
Answer.Choice_D: Provide evidence of completion of a formal 12-month head and neck surgical oncology fellowship recognized by DGHNO-KHC, with no additional CME or study documentation required
Answer.Explanation: The 2026 DGHNO-KHC/DAHNO update consolidated the training/study participation requirement for the Head and Neck Surgical Oncology expert certificate into a single pathway: applicants should document participation in or organization of oncology-focused continuing education events or studies, typically via AG Onkologie of DGHNO-KHC, DAHNO, or DSZ-HNO. Previously, applicants were required to document both at least five oncology-focused CME events and at least three clinical study/scientific activities (option B). Options C and D are not specified pathways in the 2026 guidance and do not reflect the stated requirement.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 31-year-old G2P0 woman at 12 weeks 2 days’ gestation presents for counseling about aneuploidy risk assessment after a first-trimester ultrasound demonstrated a viable dichorionic–diamniotic twin pregnancy with crown–rump lengths consistent with dates and no structural anomalies. She is healthy, takes prenatal vitamins, and prefers noninvasive testing. She will be traveling from 13 to 17 weeks and asks whether she can wait until she returns to pursue serum-based aneuploidy screening rather than arranging first-trimester testing now. According to the American College of Medical Genetics guideline issued in 2009, which of the following is recommended?

Answer.Choice_A: Offer second-trimester maternal serum screening for aneuploidy with counseling about limitations specific to multifetal gestations
Answer.Choice_B: Offer only first-trimester combined screening for aneuploidy with counseling about limitations in multifetal gestations
Answer.Choice_C: Recommend cell-free DNA screening as the preferred method for aneuploidy risk assessment in multifetal gestations
Answer.Choice_D: Recommend against aneuploidy screening and instead schedule a detailed second-trimester anatomic ultrasound alone
Answer.Explanation: The 2009 American College of Medical Genetics guideline states that in multifetal pregnancies, either first-trimester or second-trimester screening can be used, with counseling about the limitations in this setting. This expanded earlier guidance that cited only first-trimester screening as an acceptable option. Therefore, for a dichorionic twin gestation when first-trimester screening is impractical, offering second-trimester serum screening with appropriate counseling is recommended. Cell-free DNA was not recommended as the preferred approach in these guidelines, and deferring all screening in favor of ultrasound alone is not advised.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 2-month-old male is evaluated for poor feeding and hypotonia. He was born at term without complications. Family history is notable for an older maternal uncle with intellectual disability. FMR1 testing is performed. PCR fails to amplify a normal-sized allele, and Southern blot demonstrates a single expanded allele with an estimated 225 CGG repeats. Methylation analysis is pending. 

According to the American College of Medical Genetics Fragile X testing technical standards and guidelines issued in 2008, which of the following is the most appropriate classification to report for this allele?

Answer.Choice_A: Full mutation (>200 CGG repeats)
Answer.Choice_B: Premutation (report as premutation because full mutation requires >230 CGG repeats)
Answer.Choice_C: Intermediate/gray zone allele (45–54 CGG repeats)
Answer.Choice_D: Indeterminate expansion between premutation and full mutation; defer classification until methylation results are available
Answer.Explanation: The 2008 ACMG Fragile X testing standards and guidelines specify FMR1 CGG repeat categories as: Unaffected <45, Intermediate 45–54, Premutation 55–200, and Full mutation >200 repeats. An allele with 225 repeats is therefore classified as a full mutation.
