Earlier descriptions sometimes cited a >230 threshold for full mutation, creating ambiguity for alleles in the 201–230 range; the 2008 guidance standardizes the full mutation threshold at >200. Methylation status informs phenotype and counseling but does not change the size-based classification.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 52-year-old woman underwent complete surgical resection of an 8.5-cm proximal jejunal gastrointestinal stromal tumor. Pathology shows a high mitotic rate (12 per 50 high-power fields) with negative margins and no tumor rupture. Staging CT shows no metastatic disease. Molecular testing reveals a KIT exon 11 mutation. She has recovered well postoperatively and asks how long she should take adjuvant therapy to lower her risk of recurrence.

According to the Korean GIST Study Group (KGSG) clinical practice guideline issued in 2012, which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation regarding adjuvant imatinib?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate imatinib 400 mg daily as adjuvant therapy for at least 36 months
Answer.Choice_B: Initiate imatinib 400 mg daily as adjuvant therapy for 12 months
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate imatinib 400 mg daily as adjuvant therapy for 24 months
Answer.Choice_D: Initiate imatinib 400 mg daily as adjuvant therapy for 6 months
Answer.Explanation: Her tumor size, high mitotic index, and small-bowel location place her in a high-risk category after complete resection. The KGSG 2012 guideline recommends adjuvant imatinib for at least 36 months in high-risk GIST based on randomized data (SSGXVIII/AIO) showing improved recurrence-free and overall survival with 36 vs 12 months. Earlier guidance recommended at least 12 months but acknowledged this was likely insufficient for high-risk disease and that optimal duration was undetermined.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 68-year-old man presents with 24 hours of fever and shaking chills, right upper quadrant abdominal pain, and new-onset jaundice. He has a history of choledocholithiasis treated with ERCP 2 years ago. On examination, temperature is 39.2°C; scleral icterus is present with right upper quadrant tenderness. Laboratory studies show leukocytosis and elevated C-reactive protein; total bilirubin is 4.1 mg/dL with alkaline phosphatase 540 U/L and mild transaminase elevation. Ultrasound has not yet been performed. According to the Tokyo Guidelines 2013 (TG13) issued in 2012 by the Japanese Society of Hepato-Biliary-Pancreatic Surgery and the Tokyo Guidelines Revision Committee, which of the following best classifies this patient’s diagnostic status at this time?

Answer.Choice_A: Suspected acute cholangitis: one item from systemic inflammation (A) plus one item from cholestasis or imaging (B or C)
Answer.Choice_B: Definite acute cholangitis: Charcot’s triad of fever/chills, jaundice, and right upper quadrant pain is sufficient
Answer.Choice_C: Definite acute cholangitis: cholestasis (jaundice or abnormal liver tests) plus imaging evidence alone, without systemic inflammation
Answer.Choice_D: Suspected acute cholangitis: systemic inflammation alone (fever/chills or inflammatory markers) is sufficient
Answer.Explanation: Under TG13, diagnosis is categorized by A (systemic inflammation), B (cholestasis), and C (imaging). A suspected diagnosis requires one A item plus one item from B or C; a definite diagnosis requires one item from each A, B, and C. This patient has A (fever and inflammatory markers) and B (jaundice and cholestatic labs) but no imaging yet, so he meets criteria for suspected acute cholangitis. TG07 would have considered Charcot’s triad alone a definite diagnosis, but TG13 removed abdominal pain from the diagnostic items and requires the A+B+C combination for a definite diagnosis.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 78-year-old man presents with 24 hours of right upper-quadrant pain, chills, and jaundice. Temperature is 39.2 °C, heart rate 104/min, blood pressure 128/76 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. He is alert and oriented. Examination shows scleral icterus and right upper-quadrant tenderness without rebound. Laboratory studies reveal WBC count 14,600/mm3, total bilirubin 6.4 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 420 U/L, AST 86 U/L, ALT 74 U/L, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, and serum albumin 2.7 g/dL (reference 3.5–5.0). Right upper-quadrant ultrasonography shows a dilated common bile duct with an obstructing stone. He has received no antibiotics yet. According to the Tokyo Guidelines 2013 (TG13) issued in 2012 by the Japanese Society of Hepato-Biliary-Pancreatic Surgery/Tokyo Guidelines Revision Committee, which of the following approaches to severity grading is recommended at presentation?

Answer.Choice_A: Assign Grade II (moderate) because at least two prespecified clinical/laboratory criteria are met at presentation
Answer.Choice_B: Assign Grade II (moderate) only if the illness fails to respond to initial antibiotics and supportive care and no organ dysfunction is present
Answer.Choice_C: Assign Grade II (moderate) only if all five prespecified criteria are simultaneously present at presentation
Answer.Choice_D: Assign Grade II (moderate) if any one of the prespecified criteria is present at presentation
Answer.Explanation: TG13 replaces the response-to-treatment definition with prespecified objective criteria that can be applied at presentation. Grade II (moderate) acute cholangitis is defined by the presence of any two of the following: abnormal WBC count (>12,000/mm3 or <4,000/mm3), high fever (≥39 °C), age ≥75 years, total bilirubin ≥5 mg/dL, or hypoalbuminemia. This patient meets multiple criteria (age ≥75, fever ≥39 °C, WBC >12,000/mm3, bilirubin ≥5 mg/dL, low albumin) and should be classified as Grade II at presentation. Option B reflects the TG07 approach based on failure to respond to initial medical therapy. Options C and D misapply TG13; it requires any two criteria, not all five or just one.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 64-year-old man presents with 18 hours of right upper quadrant pain, fever, and jaundice. He has a history of gallstones. On arrival, temperature is 38.2°C, heart rate 96/min, blood pressure 128/74 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. He is alert and oriented without respiratory distress. Laboratory studies show WBC 11,200/µL, total bilirubin 3.1 mg/dL, AST 78 U/L, ALT 92 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 420 U/L, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, platelets 210,000/µL, and INR 1.1. Abdominal ultrasound shows a dilated common bile duct with an 8-mm obstructing distal stone. The team plans to start IV fluids and antibiotics and to arrange endoscopic biliary decompression. They want to assign a severity grade now to guide timing of intervention.

According to the Tokyo Guidelines issued in 2012 (TG13) by the Japanese Society of Hepato-Biliary-Pancreatic Surgery and the Tokyo Guidelines Revision Committee, which of the following approaches to severity grading should be used at this time?

Answer.Choice_A: Assign Grade I (mild) now at initial diagnosis because he does not meet Grade II (moderate) or Grade III (severe) criteria
Answer.Choice_B: Begin antibiotics and supportive care, then designate Grade I (mild) only if the patient responds to the initial medical treatment
Answer.Choice_C: Designate Grade II (moderate) now because leukocytosis and hyperbilirubinemia indicate worse prognosis even without organ dysfunction
Answer.Choice_D: Withhold a severity grade until after endoscopic biliary drainage because the post-drainage clinical course determines the grade
Answer.Explanation: TG13 (2012) defines Grade I (mild) acute cholangitis by exclusion at initial diagnosis: patients who do not meet Grade II or Grade III criteria are classified as Grade I. This patient has no organ dysfunction (Grade III) and does not meet TG13’s moderate criteria (eg, any two of: WBC >12,000 or <4,000; temperature ≥39°C; age ≥75 years; total bilirubin ≥5 mg/dL; low albumin). In contrast, TG07 defined mild disease retrospectively by response to initial medical therapy (supportive care and antibiotics), making option B reflect the earlier approach. Options C and D are not recommended in TG13: his values do not meet the thresholds for Grade II, and grading should not be delayed until after biliary drainage.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 72-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with fever, confusion, and right upper quadrant abdominal pain. Vital signs show temperature 39.3°C, blood pressure 84/52 mm Hg after 3 L of crystalloid, heart rate 118/min, and oxygen saturation 95% on room air. Laboratory studies show WBC 18,500/mm3, total bilirubin 6.1 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 455 U/L, and AST/ALT mildly elevated. Bedside ultrasound shows a dilated common bile duct with intraductal echoes consistent with stones. He is started on broad-spectrum antibiotics and requires a norepinephrine infusion at 0.05 µg/kg per min to maintain a mean arterial pressure ≥65 mm Hg.

According to the Japanese Society of Hepato-Biliary-Pancreatic Surgery/Tokyo Guidelines Revision Committee guideline issued in 2012 (TG13), which of the following hemodynamic requirements defines cardiovascular dysfunction for Grade III (severe) acute cholangitis?

Answer.Choice_A: Hypotension requiring dopamine ≥5 µg/kg per min, or any dose of norepinephrine
Answer.Choice_B: Hypotension requiring dopamine ≥5 µg/kg per min, or any dose of dobutamine
Answer.Choice_C: Hypotension requiring dopamine ≥10 µg/kg per min, or any dose of phenylephrine
Answer.Choice_D: Hypotension requiring vasopressin at any dose, or any dose of epinephrine
Answer.Explanation: TG13 (2012) defines cardiovascular dysfunction in Grade III acute cholangitis as hypotension requiring dopamine ≥5 µg/kg per min or any dose of norepinephrine. TG07 (2007) used dopamine ≥5 µg/kg per min or any dose of dobutamine. The update replaced dobutamine with norepinephrine as the vasopressor criterion, reflecting contemporary septic shock practice. Options C and D are plausible vasopressor thresholds but are not part of the TG13 definition.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 72-year-old woman presents with 24 hours of fever, right upper quadrant pain, and jaundice. She is febrile to 38.6°C and appears ill but alert. Blood pressure is 118/62 mm Hg and heart rate is 104/min. Laboratory studies show leukocytosis, total bilirubin 5.0 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 420 U/L, and serum creatinine 1.8 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL). In the emergency department, her urine output is 150 mL over 6 hours; she weighs 70 kg. Right upper quadrant ultrasound shows a dilated common bile duct with an obstructing stone. Broad-spectrum antibiotics and intravenous fluids are initiated.

According to the Tokyo Guidelines 2013 (TG13) issued in 2012 by the Japanese Society of Hepato-Biliary-Pancreatic Surgery and the Tokyo Guidelines Revision Committee, which of the following criteria should be used to define renal dysfunction when determining Grade III (severe) acute cholangitis in this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Oliguria or serum creatinine >2.0 mg/dL
Answer.Choice_B: Serum creatinine >2.0 mg/dL only
Answer.Choice_C: Serum creatinine >1.5 mg/dL or blood urea nitrogen >40 mg/dL
Answer.Choice_D: Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 12 hours or a doubling of serum creatinine from baseline
Answer.Explanation: TG13 broadened the renal dysfunction criterion for Grade III acute cholangitis to include oliguria in addition to serum creatinine >2.0 mg/dL. This patient’s urine output (150 mL over 6 hours at 70 kg ≈0.36 mL/kg/h) meets oliguria despite creatinine <2.0 mg/dL, satisfying the TG13 renal dysfunction criterion. TG07 required serum creatinine >2.0 mg/dL alone. Options C and D are plausible acute kidney injury thresholds but are not the TG13 severity criteria for acute cholangitis.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 27-year-old woman with physician-diagnosed asthma presents for routine follow-up. She reports brief episodes of chest tightness and wheeze about once or twice per month, usually with dust exposure. She has no nocturnal awakenings, no exercise limitation, and has not required oral corticosteroids or urgent care visits. She is not using any controller medication. Physical examination is normal, and pre-bronchodilator spirometry shows FEV1 92% of predicted with normal FEV1/FVC. She asks which inhaler she should use to relieve symptoms. According to the Saudi Initiative for Asthma guideline issued in 2012 by the Saudi Thoracic Society, which of the following reliever strategies is recommended at this treatment step?

Answer.Choice_A: Prescribe a short-acting beta2-agonist inhaler (e.g., salbutamol) to be used as needed for symptom relief
Answer.Choice_B: Prescribe a rapid-onset long-acting beta2-agonist inhaler (e.g., formoterol) to be used as needed without inhaled corticosteroid
Answer.Choice_C: Prescribe an ipratropium bromide inhaler to be used as needed as the sole reliever
Answer.Choice_D: Prescribe a low-dose budesonide–formoterol combination inhaler to be used as needed as the reliever
Answer.Explanation: Her symptoms are infrequent and mild, consistent with Step 1 treatment. The 2012 Saudi Initiative for Asthma (SINA) guideline recommends using a short-acting beta2-agonist as needed for symptom relief at this step and restricts relievers to SABA due to safety concerns and regulatory withdrawal of LABA monotherapy. In the 2009 SINA guideline, an as-needed rapid-onset LABA was an acceptable alternative, but this was removed in 2012. Options C and D are not recommended as sole relievers at Step 1 in the 2012 SINA guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 35-year-old woman with moderate persistent asthma presents with 3 months of nocturnal cough, sour taste in her mouth on awakening, and retrosternal burning after large meals. She uses an inhaled corticosteroid–long-acting beta-agonist controller and albuterol as needed. She denies dysphagia, weight loss, hematemesis, or melena. BMI is 31 kg/m^2. Lung examination is clear, and oxygen saturation is 98% on room air. She asks whether treating her reflux might help her asthma symptoms.

According to the Saudi Thoracic Society/Saudi Initiative for Asthma clinical practice guideline issued in 2012, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate a 6–8-week trial of anti-GERD measures (eg, weight loss, head-of-bed elevation, meal timing) and consider adding a proton pump inhibitor
Answer.Choice_B: Start high-dose proton pump inhibitor plus a prokinetic agent with lifestyle modifications for 6–12 weeks
Answer.Choice_C: Begin empiric high-dose H2-receptor antagonist at bedtime with lifestyle modifications for 8–12 weeks
Answer.Choice_D: Start indefinite daily high-dose proton pump inhibitor therapy without a defined trial period
Answer.Explanation: Per the 2012 Saudi Thoracic Society/Saudi Initiative for Asthma guideline, symptomatic asthmatics should receive a 6–8-week trial of anti-GERD measures and therapy; adding a PPI may be considered. Earlier guidance recommended a 6–12-week course of high-dose PPI plus a motility agent with lifestyle modifications. Options B, C, and D do not reflect the 2012 update and either use higher-intensity regimens, longer or undefined durations, or medications not recommended as first-line in this context.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 78-year-old woman presents for osteoporosis risk evaluation. She has never sustained a fragility fracture. Medical history is notable for hypertension and active tobacco use; she is not taking glucocorticoids. BMI is 22 kg/m². Physical examination is unremarkable. Using the country-calibrated FRAX tool without BMD, her 10-year probability of a major osteoporotic fracture is 15% and of hip fracture is 4.5%. She asks whether she should start medication now.

According to the International Osteoporosis Foundation and European Society for Clinical and Economic Aspects of Osteoporosis and Osteoarthritis guidance issued in 2013, which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

Answer.Choice_A: Arrange DXA BMD testing and recalculate FRAX to refine risk; defer pharmacologic treatment unless the age-specific intervention threshold is reached with BMD
Answer.Choice_B: Initiate oral alendronate now because her 10-year major osteoporotic fracture probability exceeds a 7.5% intervention threshold
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate raloxifene now because her 10-year hip fracture probability exceeds 3%
Answer.Choice_D: Provide lifestyle advice only and repeat FRAX in 10 years without BMD testing
Answer.Explanation: The 2013 IOF–ESCEO guidance sets the intervention threshold for women without prior fragility fracture at the age-specific FRAX probability equivalent to a woman with a prior fracture (“fracture threshold”), which rises with age (e.g., approximately 23% for major osteoporotic fracture at ages 75–79 in EU settings). It also recommends using lower and upper assessment thresholds to determine when to measure BMD versus treat without BMD. For this 78-year-old without prior fracture and FRAX major osteoporotic fracture probability of 15%, the value is below the age-specific intervention threshold; therefore, BMD measurement with recalculation of FRAX is appropriate rather than immediate pharmacotherapy. Earlier recommendations (circa 2008) used a largely fixed ~7.5% 10-year probability threshold for cost-effective alendronate, which would have led to treatment in this scenario. A hip fracture threshold of 3% reflects other guidelines (e.g., NOF) rather than IOF–ESCEO, and deferring reassessment for 10 years is not appropriate for a high-risk older adult.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 64-year-old woman living in Madrid presents to discuss treatment options for recently diagnosed postmenopausal osteoporosis. DXA shows a lumbar spine T-score of –2.7 and a total hip T-score of –1.8. She has no history of fragility fractures or venous thromboembolism. She discontinued oral alendronate because of severe esophagitis and declines injectable therapies. She prefers an oral, non-bisphosphonate option. According to the International Osteoporosis Foundation and European Society for Clinical and Economic Aspects of Osteoporosis and Osteoarthritis guideline issued in 2013, which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacologic option to discuss and potentially initiate?

Answer.Choice_A: Start bazedoxifene as an oral selective estrogen-receptor modulator to reduce vertebral fracture risk, with counseling regarding venous thromboembolism, leg cramps, and hot flushes
Answer.Choice_B: Start raloxifene, as it is the only selective estrogen-receptor modulator recommended for prevention and treatment of postmenopausal osteoporosis
Answer.Choice_C: Start tamoxifen to promote bone density and reduce fracture risk in postmenopausal osteoporosis
Answer.Choice_D: Start ospemifene as an oral selective estrogen-receptor modulator for fracture prevention in postmenopausal osteoporosis
Answer.Explanation: The 2013 IOF/ESCEO guideline recognizes bazedoxifene as a selective estrogen-receptor modulator approved in Europe (available in some countries such as Spain and Germany) that reduces vertebral fracture risk and, in higher-risk subgroups, non-vertebral fractures; adverse effects include increased venous thromboembolism, leg cramps, and hot flushes. The 2008 guidance listed raloxifene as the only available SERM for osteoporosis. Tamoxifen and ospemifene are not recommended therapies for osteoporosis fracture prevention in this setting.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 68-year-old woman is referred for fracture risk assessment after a screening dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) shows a femoral neck T-score of −1.3. She has a 12-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with metformin and basal insulin (HbA1c 7.5%). She is a nonsmoker, does not drink alcohol, and has no history of glucocorticoid use, rheumatoid arthritis, or prior fractures. Her BMI is 30 kg/m². You plan to estimate her 10-year fracture probability using FRAX.

According to the International Osteoporosis Foundation and the European Society for Clinical and Economic Aspects of Osteoporosis and Osteoarthritis guidance issued in 2013, which of the following best describes how diabetes should be entered into FRAX for this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Check the FRAX ‘secondary osteoporosis’ box because diabetes mellitus (including type 2) is considered a cause of secondary osteoporosis
Answer.Choice_B: Check the FRAX ‘secondary osteoporosis’ box only for type 1 diabetes; leave it unchecked for type 2 diabetes
Answer.Choice_C: Leave the FRAX ‘secondary osteoporosis’ box unchecked and account for diabetes by marking the rheumatoid arthritis box instead
Answer.Choice_D: Check the FRAX ‘secondary osteoporosis’ box only if diabetes is complicated by microvascular disease (eg, neuropathy or nephropathy)
Answer.Explanation: The 2013 IOF/ESCEO guidance expanded FRAX clinical risk factors for secondary osteoporosis to include both type 1 and type 2 diabetes, reflecting emerging evidence that type 2 diabetes confers an independent fracture risk. Therefore, ‘secondary osteoporosis’ should be selected for this patient with type 2 diabetes. Earlier guidance listed only type 1 diabetes. Rheumatoid arthritis is a separate FRAX risk factor and should not be used to represent diabetes, and the recommendation is not restricted to patients with diabetic complications.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 68-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis presents for osteoporosis risk assessment. She has been taking prednisolone 10 mg daily for the past 8 months and is expected to continue for at least several more months. Bone mineral density at the femoral neck shows a T-score of −2.1. Using FRAX (with glucocorticoid use checked), her 10-year probabilities are 18% for a major osteoporotic fracture and 3.2% for hip fracture. According to the International Osteoporosis Foundation and European Society for Clinical and Economic Aspects of Osteoporosis and Osteoarthritis guidance issued in 2013, which of the following is recommended for incorporating glucocorticoid dose into her FRAX-based risk estimate?

Answer.Choice_A: Apply dose-specific multipliers: multiply the hip fracture probability by 1.20 and the major osteoporotic fracture probability by 1.15 for prednisolone ≥7.5 mg/day.
Answer.Choice_B: Do not perform a numeric adjustment; note FRAX does not account for dose-response and rely on clinical judgment without quantification.
Answer.Choice_C: Approximate high-dose exposure by adding 5 percentage points to both FRAX probabilities.
Answer.Choice_D: Apply a uniform adjustment by multiplying both FRAX probabilities by 1.10 in all glucocorticoid users, regardless of dose.
Answer.Explanation: The 2013 IOF/ESCEO guidance introduced simple arithmetic adjustments to FRAX probabilities based on glucocorticoid dose. For high-dose exposure (prednisolone ≥7.5 mg/day), multiply the FRAX-calculated probabilities by 1.20 for hip fracture and by 1.15 for major osteoporotic fracture. The 2008 guidance noted FRAX’s lack of dose-response modeling and advised clinical judgment without a quantitative adjustment. Other fixed or additive approaches are not recommended by the guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 68-year-old woman with hypertension and symptomatic bilateral knee osteoarthritis is admitted for elective primary total knee arthroplasty. She has no history of bleeding, normal renal and hepatic function, and no contraindications to anticoagulation. She asks which medications are acceptable to prevent blood clots after surgery. According to the Korean Society of Thrombosis and Hemostasis guideline issued in 2014, which of the following is recommended for venous thromboembolism prophylaxis after total knee arthroplasty in this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate pharmacologic prophylaxis; acceptable agents include low–molecular-weight heparin, fondaparinux, dabigatran, apixaban, rivaroxaban, low-dose unfractionated heparin, warfarin, or aspirin
Answer.Choice_B: Initiate pharmacologic prophylaxis limited to low–molecular-weight heparin, warfarin, or fondaparinux; avoid other antithrombotic agents
Answer.Choice_C: Use mechanical methods alone (intermittent pneumatic compression and graduated compression stockings) for all patients; reserve anticoagulants for those who develop symptomatic thrombosis
Answer.Choice_D: Restrict prophylaxis to direct oral anticoagulants only (dabigatran, apixaban, rivaroxaban) and avoid low–molecular-weight heparin, warfarin, aspirin, or unfractionated heparin
Answer.Explanation: The 2014 Korean Society of Thrombosis and Hemostasis guideline recommends pharmacologic prophylaxis for patients undergoing total knee or hip arthroplasty with any of the following agents: LMWH, fondaparinux, dabigatran, apixaban, rivaroxaban, low-dose UFH, warfarin, or aspirin; mechanical prophylaxis is reserved for patients with a bleeding risk. Earlier guidance (2010) limited recommended agents to LMWH, warfarin, or fondaparinux. Therefore, the expanded list in choice A is correct, whereas B reflects a more restrictive earlier approach, C inappropriately applies mechanical-only prophylaxis to all patients, and D erroneously excludes several recommended options (e.g., LMWH, aspirin, warfarin, low-dose UFH).
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 67-year-old woman is admitted to the intensive care unit with septic shock due to community-acquired pneumonia. She is intubated and receiving vasopressor support, now hemodynamically improving on hospital day 2. She has a right internal jugular central venous catheter and is largely immobile. Platelet count is 220,000/µL, INR is 1.1, creatinine is 1.0 mg/dL, and there is no active bleeding. She has no history of venous thromboembolism, recent surgery, or heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. The team plans to initiate venous thromboembolism prevention. According to the Korean Society of Thrombosis and Hemostasis guideline issued in 2014, which of the following approaches to prophylaxis is recommended for critically ill patients like this one?

Answer.Choice_A: Offer either pharmacological prophylaxis (eg, low-dose UFH or LMWH) or mechanical prophylaxis (eg, intermittent pneumatic compression) as acceptable modalities
Answer.Choice_B: Offer pharmacological prophylaxis with low-dose UFH or LMWH and reserve mechanical methods (graduated compression stockings or intermittent pneumatic compression) only when anticoagulation is contraindicated
Answer.Choice_C: Routinely combine pharmacological and mechanical prophylaxis in all ICU patients to maximize efficacy
Answer.Choice_D: Withhold prophylaxis unless a high-risk threshold is met on a formal risk score or until 48–72 hours after stabilization
Answer.Explanation: The 2014 Korean Society of Thrombosis and Hemostasis guideline states that critically ill ICU patients should be assessed and offered either pharmacological prophylaxis or mechanical prophylaxis. This represents a shift from earlier guidance that prioritized pharmacological prophylaxis (low-dose UFH or LMWH) and reserved mechanical methods for contraindications to anticoagulation. Routine combined therapy for all patients or deferring prophylaxis is not recommended.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 36-year-old man is evaluated in the surgical ICU 30 hours after a high-speed motor vehicle collision. He underwent emergent splenectomy and pelvic external fixation on arrival. He is hemodynamically stable with no evidence of ongoing bleeding; his hemoglobin has been stable for the past 12 hours, his drain output is minimal, and thromboelastography is normal. He is immobilized, has a femoral central venous catheter, and currently uses intermittent pneumatic compression devices. Renal function is normal. He will likely transfer to inpatient rehabilitation later this week.

According to the Korean Society of Thrombosis and Hemostasis guideline issued in 2014, which of the following thromboprophylaxis strategies is most appropriate?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate LMWH today (approximately 30 hours post-injury) and continue pharmacologic prophylaxis for 3 months after trauma
Answer.Choice_B: Begin LMWH after primary hemostasis is achieved, using GCS or IPC if anticoagulation is contraindicated; no specified initiation window or post-discharge duration
Answer.Choice_C: Defer LMWH until hospital discharge and continue for 2 weeks as an outpatient
Answer.Choice_D: Initiate LDUH 72 hours after injury and continue only until the patient is fully ambulatory
Answer.Explanation: The 2014 Korean Society of Thrombosis and Hemostasis guideline recommends substituting pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH or LDUH) for mechanical methods once bleeding risk has been eliminated, usually within 36 hours of trauma, and continuing pharmacologic prophylaxis for 3 months after trauma (Grade 2C). This patient’s bleeding is controlled at 30 hours, so LMWH initiation now with a 3-month duration is appropriate. Earlier guidance emphasized routine prophylaxis and the use of mechanical methods when pharmacologic prophylaxis was contraindicated but did not specify a defined start window or a post-discharge duration. Options that delay initiation to discharge, wait 72 hours, or limit duration to ambulation or 2 weeks do not align with the 2014 recommendation.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A multicenter randomized trial of a new antihypertensive agent has completed enrollment and analysis in 2015. The writing group is finalizing the manuscript. One contributor, a biostatistician, developed the analysis plan, performed the primary analyses, interpreted results with the clinical team, drafted the Methods and Results, critically revised other sections, and approved the final version. When asked to sign the authorship form, he states he is willing to be accountable for the statistical components but will not agree to be accountable for all aspects of the work conducted across sites. According to the European Association of Science Editors (EASE) guidelines issued in 2014, which of the following is the most appropriate approach to authorship for this contributor?

Answer.Choice_A: List as an author only if he additionally agrees to be accountable for all aspects of the work, in addition to substantial contributions, drafting/revision, and final approval; otherwise acknowledge his contribution
Answer.Choice_B: List as an author because he already meets substantial contribution, drafting/revision, and final approval criteria, even if he declines broader accountability
Answer.Choice_C: List as an author if he agrees to be accountable only for his own analyses and is designated as the corresponding author
Answer.Choice_D: List as an author based on funding acquisition and senior supervision even without drafting or critically revising the manuscript
Answer.Explanation: EASE 2014 endorses the ICMJE 2013 authorship criteria, which add a fourth requirement: authors must agree to be accountable for all aspects of the work, in addition to substantial contributions, drafting/critical revision, and final approval. A contributor who refuses this accountability should not be listed as an author under these guidelines. Option B reflects the earlier ICMJE 2010 criteria without the accountability requirement. Options C and D describe practices not supported by EASE/ICMJE authorship standards.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 42-year-old physician–scientist recently published a randomized trial on a new community-based hypertension intervention in an English-language specialty journal read primarily by cardiologists. He has been invited to submit a full-length translated article describing the same study to a widely read public health journal in another language to reach policymakers and frontline clinicians. He has obtained written approval from the editors of both journals, and the translation will be targeted to this different audience. He asks how to document the original publication within the new article to comply with publication ethics.

According to the European Association of Science Editors guideline issued in 2014, which of the following is the most appropriate step to ethically proceed with this secondary publication?

Answer.Choice_A: Include a footnote on the title page that provides a full reference to the primary publication, in addition to the editors’ approvals
Answer.Choice_B: Proceed with the translation for a different audience after obtaining the editors’ approvals; no title page notice is necessary
Answer.Choice_C: List the primary article only in the references section without a title page note because the audiences differ
Answer.Choice_D: Mention the prior publication only in the cover letter to the receiving journal; do not include it within the article
Answer.Explanation: EASE 2014 affirms that secondary publications are permissible for a different audience with approval from both journals and adds a specific requirement to include a footnote on the title page referencing the primary article. Earlier guidance (e.g., EASE/ICMJE 2011) emphasized editor approval and audience differentiation but did not stipulate a title-page footnote. Options B–D omit or misplace the required prominently placed title-page reference, and thus do not meet the 2014 EASE requirement.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 45-year-old clinician-researcher is preparing a manuscript reporting a prespecified secondary analysis of a randomized controlled trial of a new antihypertensive. The primary outcomes and baseline characteristics of the trial were published 6 months ago in another journal. For context in the current paper, the team wishes to include the baseline characteristics table from the original article and a figure showing the previously published primary endpoint results, alongside the new subgroup analyses. The target journal follows the European Association of Science Editors recommendations.

According to the European Association of Science Editors (EASE) guideline issued in 2014, which of the following approaches to the Results section is recommended?

Answer.Choice_A: Present only new results in the Results; if inclusion of previously published data is essential for interpretation, include the minimal necessary data clearly labeled as previously published and fully cited.
Answer.Choice_B: Present only new results in the Results and exclude any previously published data entirely, even if needed for context.
Answer.Choice_C: Integrate previously published and new results in the Results without explicit labeling, provided the original article is included in the reference list.
Answer.Choice_D: Place the previously published results in a supplementary appendix and summarize them in the Results without specific citation to avoid redundancy.
Answer.Explanation: EASE updated its guidance in 2014 to state that the Results section should present new findings and that published data usually should not be included, allowing limited inclusion when essential and clearly labeled and cited. The 2011 guidance advised not including previously published data at all. Therefore, a minimal, clearly labeled, and cited inclusion when necessary aligns with the 2014 recommendation.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 72-year-old man is in the intensive care unit after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest with persistent coma and no purposeful responses despite 2 weeks of supportive care. Neurologic prognostication suggests a poor chance of meaningful recovery. After multiple family meetings, his wife and adult son agree to withdraw mechanical ventilation and focus on comfort. They live several hours away and cannot return to the hospital for at least 3 days. The treating team is prepared to complete the hospital’s life support limitation (LSL) form today.

According to the Indian Society of Critical Care Medicine/Indian Association of Palliative Care guideline issued in 2014, which of the following is the most appropriate next step regarding documentation to implement withdrawal of life support?

Answer.Choice_A: Complete the LSL form and proceed with withdrawal with treating-team signatures; obtain signatures from two or more family members if feasible, recognizing family signatures are not mandatory
Answer.Choice_B: Defer withdrawal until the LSL form is signed by two or more family members and the treating team
Answer.Choice_C: Proceed with withdrawal based only on a progress-note entry documenting verbal family agreement; an LSL form is not required
Answer.Choice_D: Seek judicial authorization before withdrawal whenever family cannot physically sign the LSL form, regardless of verbal agreement
Answer.Explanation: The 2014 ISCCM/IAPC guideline states that a family representative’s signature is not mandatory for implementing withholding/withdrawal of life support; it is preferable to have an LSL form completed and signed by two or more family members and the treating team. Earlier guidance required family signatures before implementation. Therefore, proceeding with the LSL form signed by the treating team and obtaining family signatures if feasible (without delaying withdrawal) is recommended.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 62-year-old man with a primary unilateral inguinal hernia is scheduled for elective laparoscopic transabdominal preperitoneal (TAPP) repair. He has a BMI of 23 kg/m^2, no prior abdominal surgery, and no palpable abdominal wall defects aside from the inguinal hernia. Preoperative evaluation is otherwise unremarkable. The attending surgeon is experienced in laparoscopy and has formal training in multiple access techniques, including direct trocar insertion. According to the International Endohernia Society guideline issued in 2015, which of the following is the most appropriate method to establish pneumoperitoneum in this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Direct trocar insertion at an infraumbilical site by a trained surgeon after assessing patient-specific risk factors
Answer.Choice_B: Open (Hasson) umbilical entry as the preferred alternative to Veress needle access; avoid direct trocar insertion due to safety concerns
Answer.Choice_C: Routine Veress needle entry at the umbilicus in all patients, irrespective of prior surgery or body habitus
Answer.Choice_D: Optical trocar entry without specific risk assessment or regard for the surgeon’s experience with the technique
Answer.Explanation: The 2015 International Endohernia Society guideline newly endorses direct trocar insertion (DTI) as a safe alternative to Veress, Hasson (open), or optical entry when patient-specific risk factors are considered and the surgeon is appropriately trained. In a low-risk patient without prior surgery and with an experienced surgeon, DTI is acceptable. Earlier guidance emphasized caution, use of open access as an alternative to Veress—especially after prior open abdominal surgery—and tailoring technique to patient factors, but did not specifically endorse DTI. Options C and D ignore the guideline’s requirement to individualize access and consider training.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 450-bed NHS district general hospital is revising its transfusion laboratory governance after a near-miss in which a complex antibody was first recognized at 22:30 by a band 5 biomedical scientist without immediate senior support. The pathology director asks the transfusion laboratory manager to formalize the availability of specialist transfusion advice by senior staff. The laboratory operates core hours from 08:30 to 17:00, and out-of-hours testing is provided by shift staff. The hospital can collaborate with a neighboring trust for on-call support. According to the UK Transfusion Laboratory Collaborative standard issued in 2014, which of the following service provisions should be adopted?

Answer.Choice_A: Ensure that a senior member of staff (as defined in 3.1) is available at all times to provide specialist transfusion advice, using local collaboration with other hospitals/organisations to cover non-core hours if needed
Answer.Choice_B: Ensure that a senior member of staff (as defined in 3.1) is available during non-core hours to provide specialist transfusion advice, using local collaboration with other hospitals/trusts if needed
Answer.Choice_C: Ensure that a senior member of staff is available during core hours, with out-of-hours queries directed to the national blood service advisory line
Answer.Choice_D: Ensure that on-call senior advice is limited to suspected transfusion reactions, with other queries handled by on-duty laboratory staff and reviewed the next working day
Answer.Explanation: The UK Transfusion Laboratory Collaborative standard (2014) expects senior staff, as defined in standard 3.1, to be available to provide specialist transfusion advice at all times, with local collaboration used to cover non-core hours as needed. Earlier guidance focused on ensuring availability during non-core hours only. Options C and D restrict availability and do not meet the 2014 expectation.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 120-bed rural community hospital processes about 10 type-and-screen tests per week and issues approximately 60 red blood cell units annually. The transfusion laboratory currently performs manual serologic testing during day shifts with on-call coverage overnight. After a near-miss specimen identification error, the laboratory director plans a policy update. The finance team argues that the laboratory’s low workload does not justify capital investment in automation.

According to the UK Transfusion Laboratory Collaborative recommendations issued in 2014, which of the following policies is most appropriate?

Answer.Choice_A: Implement complete walk-away automation operating 24/7 with bidirectional interfaces to the laboratory information system; until fully implemented, require documented procedures to mitigate procedural laboratory errors.
Answer.Choice_B: Because of the low testing volume and rural setting, forego complete automation and instead adopt reasonable measures to mitigate laboratory errors.
Answer.Choice_C: Implement partial automation during core hours with manual testing overnight, without a requirement for documented mitigation measures.
Answer.Choice_D: Maintain manual testing only with dual-technologist verification and plan to revisit automation in five years.
Answer.Explanation: The 2014 UK Transfusion Laboratory Collaborative guideline expects all laboratories to have complete 24/7 walk-away automation with bidirectional LIS interfaces; where complete automation is not in place, documented mitigation of procedural errors is required. Earlier guidance allowed a low-workload exception for remote/rural hospitals, which has been removed in the 2014 update.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 42-year-old man with aggressive relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis underwent autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) 5 months ago at a regional transplant center. He is clinically stable, with no infections and improving neurological symptoms. Laboratory studies show persistent mild lymphopenia without cytopenias; MRI shows no new lesions. He and his neurologist are planning his long-term post-transplant follow-up schedule and ask how often and with whom he should be seen over the next several years.

According to the European Society for Blood and Marrow Transplantation (EBMT) Autoimmune Diseases Working Party guideline issued in 2015, which of the following follow-up programs is recommended?

Answer.Choice_A: Quarterly joint consultations with both the transplant hematology and disease specialist during the first year after HSCT, then biannual joint consultations thereafter, incorporating disease-specific activity scores within a JACIE-accredited follow-up program
Answer.Choice_B: Combined care with a single joint consultation annually after day +100, with routine interim follow-up under the referring disease specialist
Answer.Choice_C: Monthly hematology-only visits for the first year, then annual visits thereafter, consulting the disease specialist only if disease activity recurs
Answer.Choice_D: Biannual joint consultations only if new symptoms arise; otherwise transition routine care solely to the disease specialist after discharge from the transplant center
Answer.Explanation: The EBMT Autoimmune Diseases Working Party (and Immunobiology Working Party) 2015 guidance recommends that AD patients managed with HSCT be followed in centers with JACIE accreditation or equivalent and receive structured long-term follow-up: quarterly consultations in the first year and biannual consultations thereafter, ideally with dual review by hematologists and disease specialists using disease-specific activity scores. Earlier EBMT recommendations (2012) specified joint annual review as a minimum after the early post-transplant period (after at least 100 days under transplant care). Therefore, the recommended program is quarterly in year 1 and biannually thereafter with dual specialist involvement.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 52-year-old clinician–researcher is preparing to submit a manuscript to the Journal of Cachexia, Sarcopenia and Muscle (JCSM). The study analyzes 12-month functional outcomes from a heart failure exercise cohort previously reported in another journal for baseline characteristics and 6-month outcomes. The new manuscript includes overlapping methods text, baseline tables, and a subset of outcomes also presented previously, along with new analyses and longer follow-up. The corresponding author asks how to proceed to align with JCSM policy before submission. According to the Journal of Cachexia, Sarcopenia and Muscle author guidelines issued in 2015, which of the following steps is recommended?

Answer.Choice_A: Reference all prior publications on the same cohort and fully describe and justify the degree of overlap and difference in the cover letter and manuscript, affirming that the submission does not contain excessive overlap
Answer.Choice_B: State in the cover letter that the work is original and is neither under consideration elsewhere nor previously published beyond abstract form, without additional discussion of overlap
Answer.Choice_C: Remove all overlapping descriptions and results even if this omits essential methods and baseline data, and avoid citing the earlier publication to prevent self-citation bias
Answer.Choice_D: Submit the manuscript without cross-referencing prior work and address any overlap only if raised during peer review, noting that limited reuse is acceptable under fair use
Answer.Explanation: JCSM’s 2015 guideline requires authors to ensure the work is original and does not contain excessive overlap with prior or contemporaneous publications; when reporting on previously published cohorts, trials, or data, authors must cite those publications and fully describe and justify the degree of overlap and difference, with unjustified excessive overlap resulting in rejection or withdrawal. The earlier (2010) policy focused on originality and the absence of concurrent or prior publication beyond abstracts without the explicit overlap disclosure and justification requirements.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 54-year-old investigator is the corresponding author of a manuscript reporting a multicenter observational study on sarcopenia in heart failure. The work was supported by a national research agency grant and an unrestricted award from a patient advocacy foundation; no industry funding was involved. He and his coauthors have no paid industry consultancies, equity holdings, or patent royalties, but two coauthors chair nonprofit guideline committees relevant to the topic. He plans to submit to the Journal of Cachexia, Sarcopenia and Muscle (JCSM) and asks what he must attest to in the submission’s authorship/disclosure statement beyond standard authorship criteria.

According to the Journal of Cachexia, Sarcopenia and Muscle guideline issued in 2015, which of the following statements should the corresponding author certify has been declared in the manuscript?

Answer.Choice_A: That all conflicts of interest (financial or otherwise) that may affect objectivity and all relevant sources of funding have been declared in the manuscript
Answer.Choice_B: That all conflicts of interest (financial or otherwise) that may affect objectivity have been declared in the manuscript, without requiring funding disclosures in the certification statement
Answer.Choice_C: That relevant sources of funding are acknowledged in a separate section, and conflict-of-interest disclosures are unnecessary in the absence of industry relationships
Answer.Choice_D: That only financial conflicts of interest have been declared, with nonfinancial conflicts and funding disclosures provided only at the editor’s request
Answer.Explanation: JCSM’s 2015 guideline requires the corresponding author to certify that both all conflicts of interest (financial or otherwise) and all relevant funding sources have been declared in the manuscript. Earlier guidance focused the certification on conflicts of interest alone. Options C and D omit required elements and/or restrict disclosures to industry or financial ties, which is inconsistent with the 2015 requirement.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 34-year-old man underwent radical orchiectomy for a 4.8-cm pure seminoma. Final pathology shows pT1 disease (tumor confined to the testis without lymphovascular invasion). Pre- and postoperative serum tumor markers are normal. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis shows no lymphadenopathy or metastatic disease. He is highly reliable and agrees to adhere to a structured follow-up schedule. According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following is the most appropriate initial post-orchiectomy management?

Answer.Choice_A: Active surveillance with a structured schedule of imaging and tumor marker monitoring
Answer.Choice_B: Adjuvant single-agent carboplatin chemotherapy
Answer.Choice_C: Adjuvant bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP) chemotherapy for two cycles
Answer.Choice_D: Primary retroperitoneal lymph node dissection
Answer.Explanation: The 2016 Saudi Oncology Society/Saudi Urological Association guideline states that for clinical Stage I seminoma, surveillance is the preferred strategy except in patients with expected poor compliance or when both high-risk features are present (primary tumor size ≥4 cm and pT2). This patient is compliant and has only one risk feature (size ≥4 cm) but pT1 disease, so surveillance is preferred. In earlier recommendations, surveillance was restricted to compliant patients with tumors <4 cm and <pT2, making adjuvant therapy (eg, carboplatin) more likely in this scenario. BEP chemotherapy and primary retroperitoneal lymph node dissection are not recommended for Stage I seminoma.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 33-year-old man underwent a right radical inguinal orchiectomy for a testicular mass. Pathology shows pure seminoma confined to the testis (pT1) with negative margins. Serum tumor markers (AFP, β-hCG, LDH) normalized postoperatively. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis shows no lymphadenopathy or metastases. He lives in a remote area and prefers adjuvant radiotherapy rather than surveillance or chemotherapy. He has had no prior scrotal or transscrotal procedures.

According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following radiotherapy fields should be used?

Answer.Choice_A: Infradiaphragmatic para-aortic strip only
Answer.Choice_B: Infradiaphragmatic para-aortic field including the ipsilateral iliac nodes
Answer.Choice_C: Infradiaphragmatic para-aortic field including bilateral iliac nodes
Answer.Choice_D: Infradiaphragmatic para-aortic field including ipsilateral iliac and inguinal canal nodes
Answer.Explanation: The 2016 Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline recommends that if adjuvant radiotherapy is selected for stage I seminoma, the field should be limited to the infradiaphragmatic para-aortic strip only, and the ipsilateral iliac nodes should be included only when prior scrotal surgery has occurred. This patient had no scrotal violation, so para-aortic only is recommended. Earlier guidance commonly included the ipsilateral iliac nodes (para-aortic ± ipsilateral iliac), a broader field now reserved for those with prior scrotal surgery due to altered lymphatic drainage.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 34-year-old man presents 3 weeks after left radical orchiectomy for a 3.2-cm pure seminoma confined to the testis. Post-orchiectomy tumor markers are normal. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen shows two left para-aortic lymph nodes measuring up to 1.8 cm; no other disease is identified. He has a 20-pack-year smoking history and moderate emphysema on pulmonary function testing. He also has ileocolonic Crohn disease with recent steroid-requiring flares. According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following is the most appropriate primary treatment?

Answer.Choice_A: Four cycles of etoposide plus cisplatin (EP ×4) systemic chemotherapy
Answer.Choice_B: Infradiaphragmatic para-aortic and ipsilateral iliac field radiotherapy
Answer.Choice_C: Three cycles of bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP ×3) systemic chemotherapy
Answer.Choice_D: Active surveillance with periodic cross-sectional imaging without immediate therapy
Answer.Explanation: This patient has stage IIA seminoma (retroperitoneal nodes ≤2 cm). The 2016 Saudi Oncology Society/Saudi Urological Association guideline states that for stage IIA/IIB seminoma, both radiotherapy to the para-aortic and ipsilateral iliac nodes and systemic chemotherapy are acceptable; radiotherapy is generally preferred for stage IIA. However, when radiotherapy is undesirable (e.g., significant inflammatory bowel disease) and there are concerns about bleomycin toxicity (e.g., emphysema, heavy smoking), chemotherapy with EP ×4 is recommended over BEP ×3. The earlier 2011 guideline emphasized radiotherapy for stage IIA and limited chemotherapy to selected stage IIB patients, making radiotherapy (Choice B) the older recommendation. BEP ×3 (Choice C) is not preferred in a patient with emphysema due to bleomycin pulmonary toxicity risk. Active surveillance (Choice D) is inappropriate for stage II disease requiring definitive therapy.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 36-year-old man presents 2 weeks after right radical orchiectomy for a testicular mass. Pathology shows pure classic seminoma. Pre-orchiectomy serum markers were: alpha-fetoprotein within normal limits, beta–human chorionic gonadotropin 120 mIU/mL, and lactate dehydrogenase 2 times the upper limit of normal. Contrast-enhanced CT reveals bulky retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (largest node 6.5 cm) and two hypodense hepatic lesions consistent with metastases. ECOG performance status is 0. Pulmonary function tests are normal. 

According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following systemic chemotherapy regimens is recommended for this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Four cycles of BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin) or four cycles of VIP (etoposide, ifosfamide, cisplatin)
Answer.Choice_B: Four cycles of BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin)
Answer.Choice_C: Three cycles of BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin)
Answer.Choice_D: Four cycles of EP (etoposide, cisplatin) without bleomycin
Answer.Explanation: This patient has metastatic seminoma with nonpulmonary visceral metastases (liver), consistent with an intermediate-risk presentation. The 2016 Saudi Oncology Society/Saudi Urological Association guideline explicitly endorses either four cycles of BEP or four cycles of VIP for intermediate-risk seminoma. Earlier guidance (2011) recommended four cycles of BEP alone. Regimens such as three cycles of BEP or four cycles of EP are appropriate for good-risk disease, not for intermediate risk.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 36-year-old man with metastatic pure seminoma presents with radiographic progression 3 months after completing second-line chemotherapy with paclitaxel, ifosfamide, and cisplatin (TIP). He initially received first-line bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP) for stage IIc disease with a partial response before relapsing 10 months later. He now reports worsening numbness and tingling in his feet consistent with grade 2 peripheral neuropathy. Physical examination shows decreased vibratory sensation in both feet; ECOG performance status is 1. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL, ANC 3,200/µL, platelets 210,000/µL, and creatinine 0.9 mg/dL. Chest and abdominal CT scans demonstrate enlarging bilateral pulmonary nodules and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. 

According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient who has progressed after second-line chemotherapy?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate oral etoposide monotherapy
Answer.Choice_B: Administer paclitaxel plus gemcitabine for patients without prior paclitaxel exposure
Answer.Choice_C: Administer gemcitabine plus carboplatin
Answer.Choice_D: Proceed to high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem-cell transplant
Answer.Explanation: The 2016 Saudi Oncology Society/Saudi Urological Association guideline expands salvage options for seminoma after failure of second-line chemotherapy to include monotherapy or combinations such as paclitaxel plus gemcitabine (if paclitaxel-naïve), gemcitabine plus oxaliplatin, and oral etoposide. In a patient who has already received paclitaxel and has grade 2 neuropathy, oral etoposide monotherapy aligns with the updated options and avoids further neurotoxic agents. The earlier 2011 recommendation focused on paclitaxel plus gemcitabine for paclitaxel-naïve patients only. Gemcitabine plus carboplatin and high-dose chemotherapy with autologous transplant are not recommended by this guideline for this setting.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 24-year-old man presents for postoperative counseling after right radical orchiectomy for a testicular mass. Pathology shows a nonseminomatous germ cell tumor, pT1, with no lymphovascular invasion. Post-orchiectomy serum tumor markers (AFP, β-hCG, LDH) have returned to normal, and CT scans of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis show no lymphadenopathy or metastases. He declines active surveillance and requests adjuvant chemotherapy but wants to minimize treatment burden and toxicity while remaining within evidence-based recommendations.

According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following adjuvant chemotherapy strategies is recommended for this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Administer a single cycle of bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP) as adjuvant therapy
Answer.Choice_B: Administer two cycles of bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP) as adjuvant therapy
Answer.Choice_C: Administer three cycles of bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP) as adjuvant therapy
Answer.Choice_D: Administer a single cycle of carboplatin (AUC 7) as adjuvant therapy
Answer.Explanation: This patient has Stage I nonseminomatous germ cell tumor without risk factors (no lymphovascular invasion, pT1, normalized markers, negative imaging). While earlier guidance recommended two cycles of adjuvant BEP, the 2016 Saudi Oncology Society/Saudi Urological Association guideline allows consideration of a single cycle of BEP in this setting, offering a shorter, less toxic adjuvant option. Two cycles of BEP (choice B) reflects the earlier standard but is not required when a single cycle is acceptable. Three cycles of BEP (choice C) is excessive for Stage I low-risk disease and reserved for higher-stage or relapsed settings. Single-agent carboplatin (choice D) is an adjuvant option for seminoma, not NSGCT.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 26-year-old man presents 3 weeks after right radical orchiectomy for a 4.2-cm testicular mass. Pathology shows a nonseminomatous germ cell tumor with predominant embryonal carcinoma and lymphovascular invasion. Surgical margins are negative. Postoperative serum tumor markers (AFP, β-hCG, and LDH) have normalized. CT scans of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis show no evidence of metastatic disease. He is physically active, has no pulmonary or renal disease, and baseline pulmonary function testing and audiometry are normal. He has no medication allergies and has reliable access to follow-up. According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate adjuvant BEP chemotherapy for two cycles
Answer.Choice_B: Refer for open nerve-sparing retroperitoneal lymph node dissection at a high-volume tertiary center
Answer.Choice_C: Manage with active surveillance with serial tumor markers and cross-sectional imaging
Answer.Choice_D: Administer a single adjuvant cycle of carboplatin (AUC 7)
Answer.Explanation: This patient has stage I nonseminomatous germ cell tumor with high-risk features (lymphovascular invasion and predominant embryonal carcinoma). The 2016 Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline recommends adjuvant chemotherapy with two cycles of BEP for such patients; open nerve-sparing RPLND is reserved only for those with a contraindication to chemotherapy and should be performed in high-volume tertiary centers. The 2011 version allowed primary RPLND as an option at high-volume centers. Active surveillance is typically considered for low-risk stage I NSGCT, and single-agent carboplatin is an adjuvant strategy for seminoma, not NSGCT.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 29-year-old man with metastatic nonseminomatous germ cell tumor (retroperitoneal and pulmonary metastases at diagnosis) completed 4 cycles of bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin. Six weeks after his last cycle, his serum alpha-fetoprotein remains elevated at 210 ng/mL (previously 1800 ng/mL) and β-hCG is 62 IU/L. CT imaging shows decreased but persistent retroperitoneal and pulmonary lesions. He has an ECOG performance status of 0 and normal renal and hepatic function. He is counseled about second-line therapy options.

According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following second-line options—specifically added compared with their 2011 guideline—is recommended for a patient with persistently elevated tumor markers after first-line chemotherapy?

Guideline citation: Saudi Oncology Society & Saudi Urological Association testicular cancer guidelines, 2016.

Answer.Choice_A: High-dose chemotherapy followed by autologous stem-cell transplant
Answer.Choice_B: Four cycles of vinblastine, ifosfamide, and cisplatin (VeIP)
Answer.Choice_C: Four cycles of etoposide, ifosfamide, and cisplatin (VIP)
Answer.Choice_D: Four cycles of gemcitabine plus oxaliplatin
Answer.Explanation: In the 2016 Saudi Oncology Society/Saudi Urological Association guideline, high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem-cell transplant was added as a recommended second-line option for metastatic NSGCT with persistently elevated tumor markers after first-line therapy, alongside VeIP×4 and TIP×4. The 2011 guideline listed only VeIP×4 or TIP×4. VIP×4 and gemcitabine/oxaliplatin are not recommended second-line regimens in these guidelines.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 34-year-old man presents 3 weeks after right inguinal orchiectomy for a testicular mass. Pathology shows classical seminoma measuring 4.6 cm, confined to the testis without lymphovascular invasion (pT1). Serum AFP was normal preoperatively; β-hCG was mildly elevated preoperatively and has normalized postoperatively. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis shows no lymphadenopathy or metastatic disease. He has reliable transportation, expresses strong commitment to follow-up, and has no barriers to care. 

According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient with clinical stage I seminoma?

Answer.Choice_A: Initiate active surveillance with scheduled tumor marker assessments and cross-sectional imaging
Answer.Choice_B: Administer a single dose of adjuvant carboplatin (AUC 7)
Answer.Choice_C: Administer adjuvant bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP) for three cycles
Answer.Choice_D: Perform a primary retroperitoneal lymph node dissection
Answer.Explanation: The 2016 Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline recommends surveillance as the preferred strategy for clinical stage I seminoma after orchiectomy, except for patients expected to be poorly compliant or those with both primary tumor size ≥4 cm and pathologic stage ≥pT2. This patient has only one risk factor (size ≥4 cm) and is pT1 with reliable follow-up; therefore, active surveillance is recommended. Earlier recommendations limited surveillance to tumors <4 cm and <pT2, favoring adjuvant therapy for larger tumors, but this has shifted to broader use of surveillance. Adjuvant carboplatin (B) or para-aortic radiotherapy were previously favored for higher-risk features; BEP chemotherapy (C) and RPLND (D) are not recommended for stage I seminoma in this context.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 32-year-old man underwent right radical inguinal orchiectomy for a 3.2-cm pure seminoma confined to the testis. Pre- and postoperative tumor markers (beta-hCG and AFP) are normal. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis shows no lymphadenopathy or metastases. He has no history of scrotal or transscrotal surgery, and the inguinal incision healed well without scrotal violation. After counseling on surveillance, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy, he elects adjuvant radiotherapy. According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which radiation field should be used?

Answer.Choice_A: Infradiaphragmatic para-aortic strip only
Answer.Choice_B: Infradiaphragmatic para-aortic field with inclusion of ipsilateral iliac nodes (dog-leg field)
Answer.Choice_C: Infradiaphragmatic para-aortic field plus bilateral pelvic (both iliac chains) nodes
Answer.Choice_D: Isolated ipsilateral iliac nodal irradiation without para-aortic coverage
Answer.Explanation: The 2016 Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline recommends para-aortic strip-only irradiation for stage I seminoma when adjuvant radiotherapy is chosen, adding ipsilateral iliac nodes only if there has been prior scrotal surgery. Earlier guidance (2014) allowed para-aortic ± ipsilateral iliac coverage more broadly. In this patient without scrotal violation, the appropriate field is para-aortic strip only (A). Options B–D irradiate pelvic nodes without the guideline-specified indication or extend fields beyond what is recommended.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 34-year-old man presents 6 weeks after a left radical orchiectomy for classical seminoma. Preoperative beta-hCG was mildly elevated and alpha-fetoprotein was normal; both have normalized postoperatively. He is asymptomatic. He has a 20–pack-year smoking history and mild COPD with a diffusion capacity (DLCO) of 45% predicted. Physical examination is unremarkable. CT of the abdomen and pelvis shows a 3.2-cm left para-aortic nodal mass without supradiaphragmatic disease; no other metastases are identified. He has normal renal function and an ECOG performance status of 0.

According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

Answer.Choice_A: Chemotherapy with four cycles of etoposide and cisplatin
Answer.Choice_B: Radiotherapy to infradiaphragmatic para-aortic and ipsilateral iliac nodes
Answer.Choice_C: Chemotherapy with three cycles of bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin
Answer.Choice_D: Radiotherapy limited to the para-aortic field without ipsilateral iliac coverage
Answer.Explanation: This patient has stage IIB seminoma (retroperitoneal nodal mass 2–5 cm) and is fit for chemotherapy but has significant pulmonary risk factors (heavy smoking and reduced DLCO). The 2016 Saudi Oncology Society/Saudi Urological Association guideline lists, for stage IIA and IIB, acceptable options of radiotherapy or chemotherapy; for stage IIB patients who are fit, systemic chemotherapy is favored, with three cycles of BEP or, when there are concerns about bleomycin lung toxicity (e.g., reduced lung capacity, emphysema, heavy smoking), four cycles of EP. Prior recommendations emphasized radiotherapy for stage IIA/IIB and reserved chemotherapy for stage IIB when radiotherapy toxicity was a concern. Therefore, four cycles of EP is most appropriate here. Radiotherapy to para-aortic and ipsilateral iliac nodes (B) reflects the earlier approach and is less appropriate in a fit stage IIB patient. Three cycles of BEP (C) is acceptable in general but is suboptimal given his elevated bleomycin pulmonary risk. Para-aortic-only radiotherapy (D) omits recommended ipsilateral iliac coverage and is not guideline-concordant.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 38-year-old man presents after right radical orchiectomy for a testicular mass. Pathology shows pure seminoma. Pre-chemotherapy staging CT reveals conglomerate retroperitoneal lymph nodes measuring 7.5 cm in the para-aortic region with no nonpulmonary visceral metastases. Serum tumor markers show β-hCG 120 mIU/mL, AFP normal, and LDH mildly elevated. He has a history of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis with a diffusion capacity of carbon monoxide (DLCO) of 45% predicted. ECOG performance status is 1 and renal function is normal. 

According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following is the most appropriate first-line chemotherapy regimen for this patient with stage IIC/III (intermediate-risk) seminoma?

Answer.Choice_A: Four cycles of VIP (etoposide, ifosfamide, and cisplatin)
Answer.Choice_B: Four cycles of BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin)
Answer.Choice_C: Four cycles of EP (etoposide and cisplatin)
Answer.Choice_D: Three cycles of BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin)
Answer.Explanation: The 2016 Saudi Oncology Society/Saudi Urological Association guideline for stage IIC/III (intermediate-risk) seminoma recommends four cycles of BEP or four cycles of VIP. In a patient with significant pulmonary disease and reduced DLCO, avoiding bleomycin is prudent; thus VIP x4 is preferred. The 2014 version recommended four cycles of BEP only. EP x4 and BEP x3 are regimens for good-risk disease and are not recommended for intermediate-risk seminoma.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 26-year-old man presents 3 weeks after right radical orchiectomy for a mixed nonseminomatous germ cell tumor (NSGCT). Pathology shows pT1 disease confined to the testis with no lymphovascular invasion. Preoperative serum tumor markers were elevated but normalized post-orchiectomy. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis shows no metastatic disease. He is reliable with follow-up and declines surveillance due to anxiety but asks to minimize chemotherapy exposure while still pursuing adjuvant treatment. According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following adjuvant chemotherapy regimens is the most appropriate?

Answer.Choice_A: One cycle of bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP)
Answer.Choice_B: Two cycles of bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP)
Answer.Choice_C: Three cycles of bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP)
Answer.Choice_D: Four cycles of etoposide and cisplatin (EP)
Answer.Explanation: For Stage I NSGCT without risk factors (no lymphovascular invasion, normalized post-orchiectomy markers, and negative imaging), the 2016 Saudi Oncology Society/Saudi Urological Association guideline states that adjuvant chemotherapy with two cycles of BEP remains an option and that one cycle of BEP can also be considered. In a patient who declines surveillance but seeks to minimize toxicity, one cycle of BEP is appropriate. The 2014 guideline endorsed two cycles of BEP for adjuvant chemotherapy; three cycles of BEP or four cycles of EP are not recommended adjuvant regimens in this low-risk, stage I setting.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 24-year-old man underwent right radical inguinal orchiectomy for a 3-cm testicular mass. Pathology shows a mixed nonseminomatous germ cell tumor composed of 70% embryonal carcinoma and 30% yolk sac tumor with lymphovascular invasion (pT2). Pre-orchiectomy serum tumor markers were elevated (AFP 250 ng/mL, β-hCG 60 IU/L) and have normalized at 4 weeks post-orchiectomy. CT scans of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis show no lymphadenopathy or metastatic disease. He has normal renal function and baseline pulmonary testing, no audiovestibular symptoms, and no contraindications to chemotherapy. He is evaluated at a high-volume tertiary center with extensive retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND) experience. He wishes to minimize relapse risk.

According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

Answer.Choice_A: Administer two cycles of adjuvant bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP) chemotherapy
Answer.Choice_B: Proceed with primary open nerve-sparing RPLND at this high-volume center
Answer.Choice_C: Initiate active surveillance with scheduled tumor markers and CT imaging
Answer.Choice_D: Administer one cycle of adjuvant BEP chemotherapy followed by surveillance
Answer.Explanation: This patient has stage I NSGCT with risk factors (lymphovascular invasion and predominance of embryonal carcinoma) and no contraindications to chemotherapy. The 2016 Saudi Oncology Society/Saudi Urological Association guideline recommends two cycles of adjuvant BEP to reduce relapse risk in this setting, and restricts primary open nerve-sparing RPLND to cases with contraindications to chemotherapy and only in high-volume tertiary centers. Earlier guidance permitted RPLND as an option in high-volume centers without requiring chemotherapy contraindication. Active surveillance or one cycle of BEP are not recommended approaches for risk-factor–positive stage I NSGCT in this guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 27-year-old man presented 8 months ago with a right testicular mass and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. He underwent radical orchiectomy, and pathology showed a mixed nonseminomatous germ cell tumor. Baseline tumor markers were elevated (AFP 1,200 ng/mL; β-hCG 1,000 IU/L). He completed 4 cycles of BEP chemotherapy. Two weeks after finishing chemotherapy, his tumor markers are increasing (AFP 350 ng/mL; β-hCG 220 IU/L), and CT scan shows a 3.5-cm residual retroperitoneal nodal mass. He has good performance status and preserved renal and hepatic function.

According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association clinical practice guideline issued in 2016, which second-line option—beyond four cycles of VeIP or four cycles of TIP—is recommended for patients with residual disease and elevated tumor markers after first-line chemotherapy?

Answer.Choice_A: High-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem-cell transplant
Answer.Choice_B: Four cycles of VeIP or four cycles of TIP
Answer.Choice_C: Surgical resection of the residual mass without further chemotherapy
Answer.Choice_D: Two additional cycles of BEP chemotherapy
Answer.Explanation: Rising AFP and β-hCG after first-line BEP in nonseminomatous germ cell tumor indicates refractory disease requiring salvage therapy. The 2016 Saudi Oncology Society/Saudi Urological Association guideline expanded second-line options for marker-positive postchemotherapy NSGCT to include high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem-cell transplant in addition to VeIP or TIP. Prior guidance (2014) listed only VeIP or TIP. Surgery alone is not appropriate when markers are elevated, and BEP rechallenge is not a recommended salvage regimen in this setting.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question: A 42-year-old man with metastatic pure seminoma underwent orchiectomy followed by first-line BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin) with an initial partial response. Six months later, he had radiographic progression and received second-line TIP (paclitaxel, ifosfamide, cisplatin). After 3 cycles of TIP, his retroperitoneal and mediastinal lymphadenopathy has increased in size, and his β-hCG has risen from 18 to 72 IU/L. He has ECOG performance status 1, normal renal function, and wishes to proceed with additional systemic therapy. According to the Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline issued in 2016, which of the following is the most appropriate third-line/salvage regimen?

Answer.Choice_A: Gemcitabine plus oxaliplatin
Answer.Choice_B: Paclitaxel plus gemcitabine
Answer.Choice_C: Repeat TIP (paclitaxel, ifosfamide, cisplatin)
Answer.Choice_D: High-dose carboplatin–etoposide with autologous stem-cell support
Answer.Explanation: The 2016 Saudi Oncology Society and Saudi Urological Association guideline expands third-line/salvage options for seminoma after failure of second-line chemotherapy to include monotherapy or combinations such as gemcitabine–oxaliplatin and oral etoposide, in addition to paclitaxel–gemcitabine only if the patient is paclitaxel-naïve. This patient has already received paclitaxel as part of TIP, making paclitaxel–gemcitabine inappropriate, and gemcitabine–oxaliplatin is a recommended option. Repeating TIP is not advised after progression on that regimen, and high-dose chemotherapy with stem-cell support is not listed as a recommended third-line option in this guideline.
Answer.Choice_E: I do not know the answer

Question:
A 52-year-old man of Northern European ancestry is referred for evaluation of iron overload. He reports fatigue and arthralgias. He drinks 2–3 beers daily. BMI is 31 kg/m^2. Physical examination shows no hepatosplenomegaly or skin hyperpigmentation. Laboratory studies show ferritin 980 ng/mL, transferrin saturation 58%, ALT 58 U/L, and AST 44 U/L. Hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis C antibody are negative. Abdominal ultrasound demonstrates hepatic steatosis. HFE genotyping identifies compound heterozygosity for p.C282Y and p.H63D. 

According to the European Molecular Genetics Quality Network (EMQN) guideline issued in 2016, which interpretive statement should be included in the genetic report for this affected individual?
